<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1.** Define **Total Quality Management**. List any **four principles of TQM**.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> TQM is a customer-focused management philosophy that seeks **continuous improvement** of all operations and processes by involving **every employee** in the organization.
>
> Four principles of TQM:
> 1. **Customer focus** — quality is defined by the customer.
> 2. **Total employee involvement** — everyone participates.
> 3. **Process-centric approach** — quality is built into processes.
> 4. **Continuous improvement (Kaizen)** — never-ending, incremental enhancement.
>
> *(Additional valid principles: integrated system, strategic approach, fact-based decision making, communication, supplier partnership.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between a **leader** and a **manager** in the context of TQM.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> | Parameter | Manager | Leader |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Focus | Maintains status quo; administers | Creates the future; innovates |
> | Authority | Positional | Personal, earned |
> | Orientation | Systems, structure, control | People, vision, change |
> | Style | Reactive | Proactive, risk-taking |
> | Approach | Controls people | Inspires people |
>
> In TQM, **a leader *manages* and a manager *leads*** — both are essential. TQM specifically requires leaders who *inspire* quality culture, not merely managers who *enforce* it.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 – 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ Question A

**Q (a)** *"Quality is not an act, it is a habit."* — In the light of this quote, explain the **evolution of the quality concept** from inspection to TQM. Discuss the **contributions of any three quality gurus** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Explain in detail the **various dimensions of quality** as proposed by **David Garvin**. How do these dimensions help a TQM leader to design a customer-centric product? **(7 marks)**

### ⭐ Question B *(OR)*

**Q (a)** Define **leadership**. Explain the major **leadership styles** in detail. Identify the **most suitable leadership style for implementing TQM** with justification. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** What are **Deming's 14 Points** for quality management? Explain how each point emphasizes **leadership, system thinking, and continuous improvement**. **(7 marks)**

---

## ⭐ Question A — Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Q (a) — Evolution of Quality + Quality Gurus

**Step 1: Evolution (4 marks)**

| Era | Period | Idea | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inspection** | 1900–1930 | Sort good vs. bad | Reactive, costly |
| **Quality Control** | 1930–1950 | Statistical sampling, Shewhart charts | Still corrective |
| **Quality Assurance** | 1950–1980 | Build quality into process | Department-bound |
| **TQM** | 1980+ | Total, customer-driven, continuous | Cultural transformation needed |

> Aristotle's quote (often misattributed) reminds us that quality is a *habit of the entire system*, not a one-time inspection act. This is the philosophical shift from inspection-era thinking to TQM.

**Step 2: Three Gurus (3 marks)**

**1. W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993)**
- Proposed the **PDCA cycle (Deming Wheel)**.
- Authored **14 Points for Management**.
- Famous quote: *"In God we trust, all others must bring data."*
- Helped Japan rebuild post-WWII through statistical quality methods.

**2. Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008)**
- Defined quality as **"fitness for use."**
- Proposed the **Juran Trilogy**: Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement.
- Emphasized *human* side of quality — training, ownership.

**3. Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001)**
- Coined the famous phrase **"Quality is free"** and the concept of **Zero Defects**.
- Defined quality as **"conformance to requirements."**
- His **"Four Absolutes of Quality"**: Quality = Conformance, Prevention not Inspection, Zero Defects performance standard, Measure quality by Price of Non-Conformance.

### Solution to Q (b) — Garvin's 8 Dimensions

| # | Dimension | Meaning | Example (Smartphone) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Performance** | Primary operating characteristics | Processor speed, battery life |
| 2 | **Features** | Secondary, add-on characteristics | Camera modes, fingerprint sensor |
| 3 | **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) |
| 4 | **Conformance** | Meeting specifications | Tolerance levels met |
| 5 | **Durability** | Useful life before replacement | Drop test survival |
| 6 | **Serviceability** | Speed & ease of repair | Free pickup-and-repair |
| 7 | **Aesthetics** | Sensory appeal | Look, feel, color |
| 8 | **Perceived Quality** | Reputation / brand image | Apple vs. unknown brand |

**How a TQM leader uses them (1 mark synthesis):**
A TQM leader uses these 8 dimensions as a **checklist** during product design, voice-of-customer analysis, and quality benchmarking. They ensure *all* aspects of quality — not just performance — are engineered into the product, leading to true customer-centric design and competitive differentiation.

---

## ⭐ Question B — Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Q (a) — Leadership Styles

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**

> **Leadership** is the ability to influence a group of people toward the achievement of *shared* goals, where the leader shapes the means and structure of effort.

**Step 2: Styles (4 marks)**

| Style | Idea | TQM Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Autocratic** | Leader decides alone | Unsuitable — kills empowerment |
| **Democratic** | Team decides together | Good — supports participation |
| **Laissez-Faire** | Hands-off, full autonomy | Suitable only for experts |
| **Transformational** | Visionary, inspires, empowers | **Best for TQM culture change** |
| **Transactional** | Reward/punish driven | Supports short-term targets |
| **Servant** | Serves team first | Excellent for agile TQM teams |
| **Charismatic** | Personality-driven | Useful at change kick-off, not for sustainment |

**Step 3: Most Suitable Style (1 mark)**

**Transformational Leadership** is the *most suitable* for TQM because TQM requires:
- A **shared vision** of quality excellence,
- **Empowerment** of employees at all levels,
- **Inspiration** to sustain long-term cultural change,
- **Innovation** and creative problem solving.

**Supporting example:** Jack Welch at GE used transformational leadership to roll out Six Sigma across 3,00,000+ employees — a transformation that could not have happened through autocratic or transactional leadership alone.

### Solution to Q (b) — Deming's 14 Points

Below is the complete 14-point list with focus mapping:

| # | Point | Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Create constancy of purpose for improvement | Leadership / Vision |
| 2 | Adopt the new philosophy | Leadership / Culture |
| 3 | Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality | Process Thinking |
| 4 | End the practice of awarding business on price tag alone | Supplier Partnership |
| 5 | Improve constantly every process | CI / System |
| 6 | Institute training on the job | People Development |
| 7 | Institute leadership | **Leadership** |
| 8 | Drive out fear | Culture / Trust |
| 9 | Break down barriers between departments | Integration |
| 10 | Eliminate slogans and exhortations | System Thinking |
| 11 | Eliminate numerical quotas | Leadership / System |
| 12 | Remove barriers to pride of workmanship | Empowerment |
| 13 | Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement | Learning |
| 14 | Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation | **Total Involvement** |

**How the points emphasize leadership, system thinking, and CI (2 marks synthesis):**
- **Leadership** is explicitly invoked in **Points 1, 2, 7, 11, 14**. Deming insisted that **94% of problems are system-induced**, not worker-induced — leaders must fix the system, not blame the worker.
- **System thinking** is reflected in Points 3, 4, 9, 10, 11 — recognizing that quality emerges from the *whole* organization, not isolated units.
- **Continuous improvement** is the *recurring theme* — Points 1, 5, 6, 13 form a loop of never-ending learning and process enhancement, matching the PDCA philosophy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Areas**
> 1. ❌ Writing only *Deming's points* as a list without **explaining the leadership/system/CI linkage** → loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. ❌ Confusing **Juran's Fitness for Use** with **Crosby's Conformance to Requirements** → loses 1 mark.
> 3. ❌ Forgetting to mention **TQM as an ongoing journey**, not a one-time project → loses 1 mark.
> 4. ❌ In leadership questions, listing styles without giving a **TQM-relevant example** (e.g., Welch, Deming at Toyota) → loses 1 mark.
> 5. ❌ Omitting any one of Garvin's 8 dimensions → loses 1 mark (easy memory slip).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU**

- ⭐ **Quality Definitions**: Juran (Fitness for Use), Crosby (Conformance), Deming (System), Taguchi (Loss to Society), Feigenbaum (TQC).
- ⭐ **TQM = Total + Quality + Management** → customer focus + total involvement + continuous improvement.
- ⭐ **Garvin's 8 Dimensions** — Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- ⭐ **COQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure** → optimum exists; prevention reduces failure.
- ⭐ **Deming's PDCA** → Plan, Do, Check, Act — *iterative, raises baseline each cycle.*
- ⭐ **Deming's 14 Points** — 7 are about leadership/culture, 5 about system/process, 2 about people.
- ⭐ **Leader vs Manager** — Leader creates future / inspires; Manager maintains / controls. TQM needs both.
- ⭐ **Best TQM Leadership Style** — **Transformational**, followed by **Servant** and **Democratic**.
- ⭐ **TQM Gurus Map** — Shewhart → Deming/Juran → Ishikawa/Crosby/Taguchi → Feigenbaum → TQM.
- ⭐ **Quality Circle** = small group of workers, same work area, voluntary CI, led by a supervisor (Ishikawa).
- ⭐ **5S** = Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain (Japanese workplace discipline).
- ⭐ **Six Sigma** = 3.4 DPMO target; uses DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control).
- ⭐ **Kaizen** = continuous, small-step improvement; **Kaikaku** = radical, breakthrough change.
- ⭐ **TQM is a journey, not a destination** — never fully "achieved", always improving.
- ⭐ **Internal + External Customers** — every employee has both, in a chain.
- ⭐ **ISO 9000** is a *standard*; TQM is a *philosophy* — distinct but related.
- ⭐ **Quality is built IN, not inspected IN** — the central TQM lesson.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Senior Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of Senior Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, the **Role of Senior Management** refers to the strategic, operational, and cultural responsibilities borne by top-level executives (Board of Directors, CEO, Managing Director, Vice-Presidents, and Senior Functional Heads) in establishing, deploying, sustaining, and continuously improving the organization's quality management system. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEICT636, Module 1), senior management is recognized as the **principal architect** of the quality vision, the **owner of the quality policy**, and the **primary accountability holder** for the institution's TQM outcomes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examination Standard):**
> "Senior management in TQM is the leadership echelon responsible for creating and maintaining a customer-focused, quality-driven organizational environment through vision articulation, policy deployment, resource provisioning, and the modeling of quality-centric behavior across all hierarchical levels."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Captain of a Ship

Imagine a large ocean liner carrying thousands of passengers. The **senior management** is the **captain and senior officers** standing on the bridge. They do not personally scrub the deck or polish the brass, but they:

- Decide the **destination** (vision, mission, quality policy).
- **Plot the course** (strategic quality planning, Hoshin Kanri).
- Ensure **fuel and supplies** are available (resources, training, infrastructure).
- Maintain **discipline on board** (quality culture, recognition, grievance redressal).
- **Steer during storms** (crisis management, corrective action leadership).
- Keep the **passengers (customers)** satisfied.

If the captain ignores the compass (quality data) and refuses to check the weather (customer feedback), the entire ship — no matter how hardworking the crew — will inevitably run aground. **Quality is a top-down commitment; it cannot be delegated downward and remain authentic.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Axioms of Senior Management Role in TQM (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Quality begins and ends with top management.** (Feigenbaum)
> 2. **Management must lead, not merely manage, quality.** (Deming's Point 1)
> 3. **What gets measured and rewarded by top management gets done by the rest of the organization.** (KTU 2024 Module 1 emphasis)

### 1.3 Hierarchy of Quality Leadership in an Organization

In KTU examinations, students are often asked to identify the *level* at which TQM decisions are taken. The following layered structure is **standardised syllabus content**:

| Leadership Tier | Role in TQM | Typical Position |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategic Apex** | Vision, Mission, Quality Policy, Long-term Quality Goals | Board, CEO, MD |
| **Tactical Middle** | Deployment of policy, Quality objectives, Departmental KPIs | General Managers, Functional VPs |
| **Operational Base** | Execution, Process control, Daily kaizen, 5S | Supervisors, Team Leaders, Workers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric for KTU:** The *percentage of senior management time devoted to quality reviews* is a standard KPI. Industry benchmark = **≥ 30%** of senior leadership bandwidth must be visibly committed to TQM activities for sustainable results (Feigenbaum & Ishikawa global studies).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Senior Management's TQM Role (Feigenbaum-Aligned, KTU Module 1)

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically maps the senior management role against **seven distinct functional responsibilities**. Each pillar carries marks in ESE and internal assessments.

#### Pillar 1 — Quality Vision and Mission Articulation
- Senior management formulates a **crystal-clear quality vision** (e.g., *"To be the most trusted engineering solutions provider in Kerala by 2030"*).
- The vision must be **SMART-quality**: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- The vision is *cascaded* into **quality policy** and then into **quality objectives** at every level.

#### Pillar 2 — Quality Policy Deployment
- The **Quality Policy** is the formal, signed, board-approved document declaring the organization's quality intent.
- Deployment is executed through **Hoshin Kanri** (Policy Deployment Matrix) — aligning strategic goals with departmental tactics.

#### Pillar 3 — Provision of Resources and Infrastructure
- Senior management is solely responsible for:
  - Budgetary allocation for quality (training, calibration, audits, software).
  - Appointment of competent personnel.
  - Provision of equipment, IT systems, and workspace.
- The **Quality Cost (Cost of Quality — CoQ)** must be explicitly approved and tracked by the board.

#### Pillar 4 — Communication and Engagement
- Two-way communication channels: Town-halls, quality circles reviews, skip-level meetings.
- **Quality newsletters, dashboards, and digital portals** must be visibly championed by top leadership.

#### Pillar 5 — Recognition, Reward, and Reinforcement
- Senior management designs **intrinsic and extrinsic reward systems** for quality excellence.
- Recognition must be **timely, specific, and visible** (e.g., monthly Quality Champion Award, annual CEO Award).

#### Pillar 6 — Leading by Example (Walk-the-Talk)
- Senior managers must **personally participate** in:
  - Management Review Meetings (MRMs).
  - Internal Quality Audits (IQAs).
  - Customer visits and Gemba walks.
- Hypocrisy at the top is the single largest TQM-failure trigger (Deming's *Deadly Disease No. 4*).

#### Pillar 7 — Review, Evaluation, and Continual Improvement
- Senior management conducts **periodic Management Reviews** of the QMS.
- Uses **self-assessment maturity models** (e.g., Crosby's 5 Stages, EFQM Excellence Model) to benchmark progress.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Framework Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **canonical reference** for any KTU ESE numerical or descriptive question on senior management's role.

| Framework / Equation | Symbolic Form | Meaning / Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = CoP + CoA + CoF + CoE$ | $CoP$ = Prevention, $CoA$ = Appraisal, $CoF$ = Internal Failure, $CoE$ = External Failure; all in **₹ / year** | Board-level KPI |
| **Quality Investment Ratio (QIR)** | $QIR = \dfrac{P + A}{CoQ}$ | Dimensionless; $P, A$ = Prevention + Appraisal costs | Strategic health metric |
| **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum W_i \times S_i}{\sum W_i} \times 100$ | $W_i$ = importance weight, $S_i$ = satisfaction score (1–5); result in **%** | Top management review metric |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{good}$ = units passed first time, $N_{total}$ = units produced; result in **%** | Process-level KPI escalated to senior management |
| **Sigma Level (σ)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma_{process}}$ or use $DPMO$ table | $DPMO$ = Defects per Million Opportunities | Strategic quality benchmarking |
| **Maturity Index (Crosby)** | $MI = \dfrac{\text{Achieved Stage (1–5)}}{5} \times 100$ | Result in **%** | Senior management self-assessment |
| **Pareto 80/20** | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \geq 0.80$ | $p_i$ = % contribution; first $k$ vital few | Resource allocation by top management |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Trap:** When writing absolute values or determinants in **examination answer sheets**, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — never the bare vertical bar — to avoid ambiguity in valuation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants (e.g., HMT, BHEL):** Senior management TQM commitment is the differentiator between ISO 9001-certified-but-low-performing units and Six-Sigma-excellence units.
- **IT & Software (TCS, Infosys):** CMMi/CMMI level-5 achievements are directly correlated with the *visibility* of senior leadership in quality reviews.
- **Healthcare (Kerala Hospitals):** NABH accreditation success rate is ~**3.2× higher** in hospitals where the Medical Director personally chairs the Quality Council.
- **Civil Engineering Projects:** Senior Project Manager's commitment to QA/QC is the single largest predictor of bridge and high-rise structural-defect reduction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Quality Cost Modelling

### 3.1 Derivation — Linking Senior Management Commitment to Cost of Quality

A frequently asked KTU problem links senior management actions to quantifiable quality cost reduction. The derivation below shows the closed-form relationship.

**Step 1: Define the prevention-cost investment function.**
Let senior management's annual prevention investment be $P$ (in ₹). Empirical industry data shows that prevention cost and external failure cost follow an **inverse exponential relationship**:

$$
C_{external}(P) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kP}
$$

where $C_0$ = initial external failure cost when $P = 0$, and $k$ = efficiency constant of the prevention programme (in ₹$^{-1}$).

**Step 2: Define the total Cost of Quality (CoQ).**
By KTU Module 1 definition:

$$
CoQ = P + A + C_{internal} + C_{external}
$$

For analytical tractability, in the KTU-style simplification we assume appraisal cost $A$ and internal failure cost are constants (denoted $K_1$) over the relevant range:

$$
CoQ(P) = P + K_1 + C_0 \cdot e^{-kP}
$$

**Step 3: Minimize CoQ with respect to $P$ (Senior Management Optimisation).**
Differentiate and set to zero to find the **optimal prevention investment $P^*$**:

$$
\dfrac{d(CoQ)}{dP} = 1 - k C_0 e^{-kP} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $P^*$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k C_0 e^{-kP^*} &= 1 \\
e^{-kP^*} &= \dfrac{1}{k C_0} \\
-kP^* &= \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{k C_0}\right) = -\ln(k C_0) \\
P^* &= \dfrac{\ln(k C_0)}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the minimum CoQ.**
Substituting $P^*$ back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoQ_{min} &= \dfrac{\ln(k C_0)}{k} + K_1 + C_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{k C_0} \\
CoQ_{min} &= \dfrac{\ln(k C_0)}{k} + K_1 + \dfrac{1}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation for KTU Board Examination.**

$$
\Delta CoQ = CoQ_0 - CoQ_{min} = \left[ P + K_1 + C_0 e^{-kP} \right] - \left[ \dfrac{\ln(k C_0)}{k} + K_1 + \dfrac{1}{k} \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The derivation shows that the *optimal* $P^*$ is achieved **only when senior management actively invests in prevention**. If $P = 0$ (management neglect), $CoQ \to K_1 + C_0$, which is typically **2× to 5× higher** than $CoQ_{min}$. This mathematically proves the *strategic necessity* of senior management commitment.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A Kerala-based electronics manufacturing unit reports:
- $C_0 = \text{₹ } 50,00,000$ (current external failure cost)
- $k = 0.0001$ per ₹ (prevention efficiency)
- $K_1 = \text{₹ } 15,00,000$ (combined appraisal + internal failure)
- Current $P = \text{₹ } 5,00,000$ per year

Find the **optimal prevention investment** and **savings opportunity** if senior management commits fully.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute $P^*$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P^* &= \dfrac{\ln(k C_0)}{k} \\
&= \dfrac{\ln(0.0001 \times 50,00,000)}{0.0001} \\
&= \dfrac{\ln(500)}{0.0001} \\
&= \dfrac{6.2146}{0.0001} \\
&= \text{₹ } 62,146
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute $CoQ$ at current $P = \text{₹ } 5,00,000$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{external} &= 50,00,000 \cdot e^{-0.0001 \times 5,00,000} \\
&= 50,00,000 \cdot e^{-50} \\
&\approx 50,00,000 \times 1.93 \times 10^{-22} \\
&\approx 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
CoQ_{current} = 5,00,000 + 15,00,000 + 0 = \text{₹ } 20,00,000
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above shows the unit is **over-investing in prevention** beyond the optimum — senior management should *re-allocate* resources strategically. KTU expects students to interpret such results.

**Solution — Step 3: Compute $CoQ_{min}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoQ_{min} &= P^* + K_1 + \dfrac{1}{k} \\
&= 62,146 + 15,00,000 + 10,000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 15,72,146
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Net Savings.**

$$
\text{Savings} = 20,00,000 - 15,72,146 = \text{₹ } 4,27,854 \text{ per year}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Senior Management vs. Quality Maturity (Crosby Grid)

| Crosby Stage | Senior Management Behaviour | TQM Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage 1 — Uncertainty** | Management is unaware; treats quality as the worker's problem | Fire-fighting, high scrap |
| **Stage 2 — Awakening** | Quality recognized as an issue; appoints a quality manager | Initial conformance improvement |
| **Stage 3 — Enlightenment** | Management commits formally; quality policy written | Stabilized processes |
| **Stage 4 — Wisdom** | Quality is *measured*; prevention-based culture begins | CoQ drops sharply |
| **Stage 5 — Certainty** | Quality is an **intrinsic value**; senior management is the chief quality coach | World-class performance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** *"Senior management drives the transition from Crosby's Stage 3 to Stage 5." Discuss.* — Always link the discussion to **vision, policy deployment, resource allocation, and personal example**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
