- **Definition (2 marks):** *MTTF is the average (arithmetic mean) time elapsed before a non-repairable component fails for the first time. For an exponential failure distribution with constant failure rate $\lambda$, MTTF = $1/\lambda$.*
- **Difference (1 mark):** *MTBF applies to repairable systems and represents the average time between successive failures. Mathematically, MTBF = MTTF + MTTR. MTTF is computed only for non-repairable items.*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the relationship between MTTF and the failure rate $\lambda$ for a system exhibiting constant failure rate. What is the corresponding reliability function?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Relationship (2 marks):** *For constant failure rate, $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$, where $\lambda$ is the constant failure rate in failures per hour.*
- **Reliability function (1 mark):** *$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$, expressing the probability that the item survives past time `t`.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:**
> Students often write "MTTF = MTBF" for repairable systems and lose **2–3 marks instantly**. The examiner's key explicitly distinguishes them. **Always identify the system type first.**
>
> **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert units (e.g., minutes to hours) when using $\lambda$. A capacitor with $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/minute has $\text{MTTF} = 1000$ min = **16.67 hours**, not 1000 hours.
>
> **Common Mistake #3:** Writing the formula $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt$ without stating its derivation or the integration-by-parts step. Examiners allocate **1 full mark** for the derivation link.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**A company manufactures LED bulbs. Quality control records show that during the useful life phase, the LED bulb failure rate is constant at $\lambda = 0.00025$ failures per hour.**

**(a)** Compute the **MTTF** of the LED bulb and interpret it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If a consumer uses the LED bulb for **3 years continuously** (assume 1 year = 8760 hours), determine: **(i)** the reliability $R(t)$ at 3 years, **(ii)** the probability of failure within 3 years, and **(iii)** the number of bulbs expected to fail out of a batch of 10,000 bulbs in this period. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the Distribution.**
The failure rate is stated as *constant*, so the exponential model applies (Useful-Life phase of the bathtub curve).

**Step 2 — Apply the MTTF Formula.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.00025 \text{ failures/hour}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Value.**

$$\text{MTTF} = 4000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Days for Interpretation (KTU loves unit conversion).**

$$\text{MTTF in days} = \frac{4000}{24} \approx 166.67 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation (1 mark reserved).**
*On average, an LED bulb is expected to operate for 4000 hours (≈ 167 days of continuous use) before failing. This MTTF is a critical input to the company's warranty policy: if the standard warranty is 1 year (8760 hours), the bulb is **more reliable than warranted** by a factor of 8760/4000 ≈ 2.19, allowing the company to either reduce the warranty reserve or extend coverage to gain a marketing edge.*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $t = 3 \text{ years} = 3 \times 8760 = 26{,}280 \text{ hours}$. $\lambda = 0.00025$/hr.

**Step (i) — Compute Reliability R(t).**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.00025 \times 26280}$$

$$R(t) = e^{-6.57}$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$R(t) = e^{-6.57} \approx 0.00141$$

**Interpretation:** Only about **0.141%** of LED bulbs survive beyond 3 years of continuous operation. This is realistic because the continuous-use scenario is extremely demanding.

**Step (ii) — Compute Probability of Failure within 3 Years.**

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - 0.00141 \approx 0.99859$$

So the probability of failure within 3 years is approximately **99.86%**.

**Step (iii) — Expected Number of Failures in a Batch of 10,000.**

$$E[\text{Failures}] = N \cdot F(t) = 10{,}000 \times 0.99859 \approx 9986 \text{ bulbs}$$

**Engineering Conclusion:**
*Out of 10,000 LED bulbs used continuously for 3 years, about 9,986 are expected to fail. The TQM team should set the warranty period based on this probabilistic expectation, not on optimistic point estimates. A more realistic policy is to offer a warranty of, say, 1500 hours, at which:*

$$R(1500) = e^{-0.00025 \times 1500} = e^{-0.375} \approx 0.687$$

*So 68.7% of bulbs survive past 1500 hours, giving a manageable warranty cost while maintaining customer trust.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**A TQM study on a batch of 800 ball bearings yields the following grouped failure data:**

| Time Interval (hours) | Number of Failures $n_i$ |
|----------------------|---------------------------|
| 0 – 500 | 80 |
| 500 – 1000 | 160 |
| 1000 – 1500 | 200 |
| 1500 – 2000 | 180 |
| 2000 – 2500 | 100 |
| 2500 – 3000 | 80 |

**(a)** Calculate the **MTTF** of the bearings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Assuming the failure rate is approximately constant (useful-life phase), determine the **failure rate $\lambda$** and the **reliability at $t = 1000$ hours**. Also estimate how many bearings out of 800 are expected to survive beyond 1000 hours. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute Bin Midpoints and Frequencies.**

| Interval | Midpoint $t_i$ | $n_i$ | $f_i = n_i / 800$ | $f_i \cdot t_i$ |
|----------|----------------|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| 0–500 | 250 | 80 | 0.10 | 25 |
| 500–1000 | 750 | 160 | 0.20 | 150 |
| 1000–1500 | 1250 | 200 | 0.25 | 312.5 |
| 1500–2000 | 1750 | 180 | 0.225 | 393.75 |
| 2000–2500 | 2250 | 100 | 0.125 | 281.25 |
| 2500–3000 | 2750 | 80 | 0.10 | 275.0 |

**Step 2 — Sum the Frequencies as a Sanity Check.**

$$\sum f_i = 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.225 + 0.125 + 0.10 = 1.000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Weighted Midpoints (Process Marks).**

$$\sum f_i \cdot t_i = 25 + 150 + 312.5 + 393.75 + 281.25 + 275.0$$

**Step 4 — Final Addition.**

$$\text{MTTF} = 1437.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — State the Conclusion.**
*The mean time to failure of the ball bearings is **1437.5 hours** (approximately 60 days of continuous operation).*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the Failure Rate $\lambda$.**
Assuming constant failure rate:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{1437.5} \approx 6.957 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours.**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 1000} = e^{-0.0006957 \times 1000} = e^{-0.6957}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Numerically.**

$$R(1000) \approx 0.4987$$

**Step 4 — Compute Expected Survivors out of 800 Bearings.**

$$E[\text{Survivors}] = N \cdot R(1000) = 800 \times 0.4987 \approx 399 \text{ bearings}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Expected Failures (Sanity Check).**

$$E[\text{Failures}] = 800 - 399 = 401 \text{ bearings}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
*At 1000 hours, the reliability is only about 50%, meaning the bearing has reached its "half-life." A preventive maintenance schedule that replaces bearings at $t \approx 1000$ hours would be near-optimal from a TQM perspective — it avoids both infant-mortality failures (early) and wear-out failures (late).*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Pin These to Your Wall Before the Exam**

- **Definition Tier:**
  - MTTF = Mean Time To Failure (non-repairable items only).
  - MTBF = MTTF + MTTR (repairable systems only).
  - MTTR = Mean Time To Repair.

- **Core Formulas (Memorize in this exact order):**
  - $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (exponential model)
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$
  - $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt$ (general)
  - $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ (empirical sample mean)
  - $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ (availability of repairable system)

- **Distribution Caveats:**
  - Exponential ⇒ **constant** $\lambda$ (Useful-Life region of the bathtub curve).
  - Weibull with $\beta < 1$ ⇒ Infant Mortality (decreasing $\lambda$).
  - Weibull with $\beta > 1$ ⇒ Wear-Out (increasing $\lambda$).
  - When in doubt, **assume exponential / Useful-Life phase** — that is the KTU default convention.

- **Unit Conversions to Master:**
  - 1 year = 8760 hours, 1 FIT = 1 failure / 10⁹ device-hours.
  - $\lambda$ in /hr × 8760 = failures per year.
  - $\text{MTTF in years} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF in hours}}{8760}$.

- **TQM Decision Links:**
  - Long MTTF → extended warranty possible → competitive advantage.
  - Short MTTF → higher spare inventory → higher carrying cost.
  - MTTF/MTBF/MTTR triad is the **inputs to Six-Sigma yield calculations** and to **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** in Total Productive Maintenance (TPM).

- **Common Examiner Traps:**
  - Mixing up MTBF and MTTF (lose 2–3 marks).
  - Forgetting to identify the system as repairable vs. non-repairable.
  - Using exponential formula in a wear-out problem (no constant $\lambda$ there).
  - Skipping the integration-by-parts derivation when the question asks "show that MTTF = $\int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$."

- **One-Line Mantra for the Exam Hall:**
  *"Non-repairable → MTTF = 1/λ ; Repairable → MTBF = MTTF + MTTR ; Useful Life → Exponential applies."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Tools: The Seven Tools of Quality, Statistical Fundamentals, Measures of Central Tendency and Dispersion, Population and Sample, and Normal Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quality Tools: The Seven Tools of Quality, Statistical Fundamentals, Measures of Central Tendency and Dispersion, Population and Sample, and Normal Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Seven Tools of Quality — Formal Definition

The **Seven Tools of Quality** (also known as **Ishikawa's Seven Basic Quality Tools** or the **7QC Tools**) are a fixed set of graphical and statistical techniques used by quality professionals to troubleshoot, analyze, and improve production and business processes. The framework was popularized by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in 1968, and the tools are derived from statistical process control (SPC) philosophy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEICT636 — Module 2):** *The Seven Tools of Quality are Cause-and-Effect Diagrams, Check Sheets, Control Charts, Histograms, Pareto Charts, Scatter Diagrams, and Flowcharts. These are the seven visual aids most commonly used in Total Quality Management (TQM) to identify root causes of variation, monitor processes, and support continuous improvement.*

The seven tools are classified into two groups based on the nature of data they handle:

| Group | Tools | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantitative Tools** | Histogram, Pareto Chart, Control Chart, Scatter Diagram | Analyze numerical/process data |
| **Qualitative Tools** | Cause-and-Effect Diagram, Check Sheet, Flowchart | Analyze language-based/process flow data |

## 1.2 Statistical Fundamentals — Definitions

**Statistics** is the science of collecting, organizing, analyzing, interpreting, and presenting data to support decision-making. In TQM, statistics forms the **empirical backbone** of quality control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Branches of Statistics:**
> 1. **Descriptive Statistics** — Summarizes data (mean, median, mode, range, standard deviation).
> 2. **Inferential Statistics** — Draws conclusions about a population from a sample (hypothesis testing, regression).

## 1.3 Measures of Central Tendency

A **Measure of Central Tendency** is a single value that attempts to describe a set of data by identifying the **central position** within that set. The three classical measures are:

- **Mean (Arithmetic Average)** — The sum of all values divided by the number of observations.
- **Median** — The middle value when data is arranged in ascending/descending order.
- **Mode** — The most frequently occurring value in the dataset.

## 1.4 Measures of Dispersion

A **Measure of Dispersion** (or variability/spread) quantifies the extent to which numerical values in a dataset tend to vary about an average value. The principal measures are:

- **Range** — Difference between maximum and minimum values.
- **Variance (σ²)** — Average of the squared deviations from the mean.
- **Standard Deviation (σ)** — Square root of variance; the most widely used dispersion metric.

## 1.5 Population vs. Sample

- **Population (N):** The entire group of individuals, items, or measurements about which information is desired.
- **Sample (n):** A subset of the population selected for actual study.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Rule (KTU Convention):**
> - Population parameters are denoted by **Greek letters**: μ (mean), σ (standard deviation), σ² (variance).
> - Sample statistics are denoted by **Latin letters**: $\bar{x}$ (mean), s (standard deviation), s² (variance).

## 1.6 The Normal Curve

The **Normal Distribution** (also called the **Gaussian Distribution** or **Bell Curve**) is a continuous probability distribution symmetric about its mean, where the frequency of observations is highest near the mean and tapers off symmetrically toward both tails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule):** For a normal distribution, approximately **68%** of data lies within ±1σ, **95%** within ±2σ, and **99.7%** within ±3σ of the mean.

---

## 1.7 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Classroom Exam Analogy"**
>
> Imagine a teacher gives a math test to **500 students** (population). Checking each paper is impossible, so the teacher picks **50 random papers** (sample) and calculates the **average score (mean)**, **middle score (median)**, and **most common score (mode)** to get a feel for the whole class. She then measures how much scores **spread out** (standard deviation) — a small spread means the class performed uniformly; a large spread means mixed performance.
>
> When she plots the **histogram of scores**, the shape resembles a **bell curve (Normal Distribution)** — most students cluster around the average, with a few high and low outliers. The **Pareto Chart** would show that 80% of low scorers failed on just 2–3 questions (the "vital few"). The **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** helps her brainstorm *why* those specific questions went wrong (poor teaching, tricky wording, time pressure). This is exactly how TQM practitioners use the **Seven Tools** in manufacturing or service industries.

---

## 1.8 Key Highlights for KTU Examination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - Carries **high weightage** in ESE (End Semester Examination) Module 2.
> - Frequently tested as **Part A (3 marks)** — definition-based or formula-based.
> - Part B questions often involve **calculation problems** on mean, median, mode, variance, standard deviation, and normal curve probability.
> - The Seven Tools are **theory-intensive** — students must know each tool's purpose, diagram, and use case.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Tools of Quality — Detailed Analysis

### Tool 1: Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone Diagram)

- **Purpose:** Identifies the potential root causes of a problem (effect).
- **Structure:** A central horizontal "spine" with branches representing major cause categories.
- **Standard Categories (6M):** **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment)**.
- **Use Case:** Brainstorming sessions, root cause analysis (RCA), Kaizen workshops.

> [!NOTE]
> *When to use:* When a problem is complex and requires identification of multiple contributing causes.

### Tool 2: Check Sheet

- **Purpose:** A structured form for **systematic data collection** in real-time at the location where data is generated.
- **Structure:** Pre-formatted表格 with categories, tally marks, and timestamps.
- **Use Case:** Defect tracking, frequency of occurrences, location of defects.

### Tool 3: Control Chart (Shewhart Chart)

- **Purpose:** Monitors a process **over time** to determine whether it is in a state of statistical control.
- **Structure:** Time on X-axis, process variable on Y-axis, with three lines: **Center Line (CL)**, **Upper Control Limit (UCL)**, and **Lower Control Limit (LCL)**.
- **Use Case:** SPC (Statistical Process Control), detecting process drift.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Limits (3σ Limits):**
> - UCL = μ + 3σ
> - LCL = μ − 3σ
> - CL = μ

### Tool 4: Histogram

- **Purpose:** Shows the **frequency distribution** of a dataset in graphical form (bars).
- **Structure:** Continuous variable on X-axis, frequency (count) on Y-axis.
- **Use Case:** Visualizing shape of distribution, central tendency, and spread.

### Tool 5: Pareto Chart

- **Purpose:** Combines a bar chart (descending frequencies) with a cumulative percentage line.
- **Principle:** The **80/20 Rule (Pareto Principle)** — 80% of problems are caused by 20% of causes ("vital few vs. trivial many").
- **Use Case:** Prioritization of problems/defects.

### Tool 6: Scatter Diagram

- **Purpose:** Plots pairs of numerical data to reveal the **correlation** (relationship) between two variables.
- **Correlation Types:** Positive, Negative, No correlation.
- **Use Case:** Investigating cause-effect relationships between two variables.

### Tool 7: Flowchart

- **Purpose:** A pictorial representation showing the **sequence of steps** in a process.
- **Standard Symbols:** Oval (Start/End), Rectangle (Process), Diamond (Decision), Arrow (Flow).
- **Use Case:** Process mapping, SOP documentation, identifying bottlenecks.

---

## 2.2 Statistical Fundamentals — Detailed Analysis

### Types of Data

| Type | Sub-Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative (Categorical)** | Nominal, Ordinal | Color, Customer satisfaction rating |
| **Quantitative (Numerical)** | Discrete, Continuous | Number of defects, Length, Weight |

### Types of Sampling

- **Random Sampling** — Every member has equal chance.
- **Stratified Sampling** — Population divided into strata (subgroups).
- **Systematic Sampling** — Every kth item selected.
- **Cluster Sampling** — Random clusters selected.
- **Convenience Sampling** — Easily accessible members.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of calculation questions in KTU ESE.**

| Concept | Formula | Notation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic Mean (Population)** | $\mu = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{N}$ | N = population size | Average of entire population |
| **Arithmetic Mean (Sample)** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | n = sample size | Average of sample data |
| **Median (Odd n)** | Median = $\left(\dfrac{n+1}{2}\right)^{\text{th}}$ value | Data sorted ascending | Middle value |
| **Median (Even n)** | Median = average of $(n/2)^{\text{th}}$ and $(n/2 + 1)^{\text{th}}$ values | — | Middle two averaged |
| **Mode** | Most frequent value | — | Peak of distribution |
| **Range** | R = X$_{max}$ − X$_{min}$ | — | Crude spread measure |
| **Variance (Population)** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}$ | — | Average squared deviation |
| **Variance (Sample)** | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n - 1}$ | Bessel's correction | Unbiased estimator |
| **Std. Deviation (Population)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}}$ | — | Spread in same unit as data |
| **Std. Deviation (Sample)** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n - 1}}$ | — | Unbiased estimator |
| **Z-Score** | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | — | Standardized value |
| **Normal PDF** | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | — | Probability density |
| **Empirical Rule** | 68% within ±1σ, 95% within ±2σ, 99.7% within ±3σ | — | Quick probability estimate |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER use the `|` character inside a markdown table.** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation in LaTeX to avoid breaking the table rendering.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (Six Sigma):** The Seven Tools + Normal Curve are foundational to **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology.
- **Service Industry (Banking, Healthcare):** Histograms and Pareto Charts help prioritize customer complaints.
- **Software Engineering:** Control Charts monitor defect arrival rates in CI/CD pipelines; Scatter Diagrams correlate code complexity vs. defect density.
- **Process Capability (Cp, Cpk):** Built on Normal Distribution assumptions to assess if a process meets specification limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Mean, Median, Mode Calculation

**Problem:** The diameters (in mm) of 10 piston rings produced by a CNC machine are: 50.2, 50.5, 50.3, 50.6, 50.4, 50.5, 50.7, 50.5, 50.6, 50.3. Calculate (a) Mean, (b) Median, (c) Mode.

### (a) Mean Calculation

**Step 1:** Arrange the data and compute the sum.

Data (raw): 50.2, 50.5, 50.3, 50.6, 50.4, 50.5, 50.7, 50.5, 50.6, 50.3

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 50.2 + 50.5 + 50.3 + 50.6 + 50.4 + 50.5 + 50.7 + 50.5 + 50.6 + 50.3 \\
&= (50.2 + 50.3 + 50.3) + (50.4 + 50.5 + 50.5 + 50.5) + (50.6 + 50.6) + 50.7 \\
&= 150.8 + 151.9 + 101.2 + 50.7 \\
&= 504.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Divide by the number of observations (n = 10).

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n} = \dfrac{504.6}{10} = 50.46 \text{ mm}
$$

### (b) Median Calculation

**Step 1:** Sort the data in ascending order.

Sorted: 50.2, 50.3, 50.3, 50.4, 50.5, 50.5, 50.5, 50.6, 50.6, 50.7

**Step 2:** Since n = 10 (even), the median is the average of the 5th and 6th values.

$$
\text{Median} = \dfrac{x_{(5)} + x_{(6)}}{2} = \dfrac{50.5 + 50.5}{2} = 50.5 \text{ mm}
$$

### (c) Mode Calculation

**Step 1:** Identify the most frequently occurring value.

Frequency table:
- 50.2 → 1 time
- 50.3 → 2 times
- 50.4 → 1 time
- 50.5 → **3 times** ← highest
- 50.6 → 2 times
- 50.7 → 1 time

$$
\text{Mode} = 50.5 \text{ mm}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Variance & Standard Deviation

**Problem:** Using the same data set, calculate the (a) Population Variance, (b) Population Standard Deviation, (c) Sample Variance, (d) Sample Standard Deviation.

### Step 1: Compute deviations from the mean ($\bar{x}$ = 50.46).

| $x_i$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 50.2 | −0.26 | 0.0676 |
| 50.3 | −0.16 | 0.0256 |
| 50.3 | −0.16 | 0.0256 |
| 50.4 | −0.06 | 0.0036 |
| 50.5 | +0.04 | 0.0016 |
| 50.5 | +0.04 | 0.0016 |
| 50.5 | +0.04 | 0.0016 |
| 50.6 | +0.14 | 0.0196 |
| 50.6 | +0.14 | 0.0196 |
| 50.7 | +0.24 | 0.0576 |
| **Sum** | 0.00 | **0.2240** |

### (a) Population Variance (using N = 10)

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N} = \dfrac{0.2240}{10} = 0.0224 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### (b) Population Standard Deviation

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{0.0224} = 0.1497 \approx 0.15 \text{ mm}
$$

### (c) Sample Variance (using n − 1 = 9, Bessel's correction)

$$
s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n - 1} = \dfrac{0.2240}{9} = 0.02489 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### (d) Sample Standard Deviation

$$
s = \sqrt{0.02489} = 0.1578 \approx 0.158 \text{ mm}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Normal Curve Probability (Empirical Rule)

**Problem:** A packaging machine fills rice bags. The weight follows a normal distribution with μ = 50 kg and σ = 2 kg. Find the probability that a randomly selected bag weighs between 46 kg and 54 kg.

### Step 1: Convert limits to Z-scores.

$$
Z_1 = \dfrac{x_1 - \mu}{\sigma} = \dfrac{46 - 50}{2} = \dfrac{-4}{2} = -2
$$

$$
Z_2 = \dfrac{x_2 - \mu}{\sigma} = \dfrac{54 - 50}{2} = \dfrac{4}{2} = +2
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Empirical Rule.

The interval [46, 54] corresponds to [μ − 2σ, μ + 2σ], which by the **Empirical Rule** contains approximately **95%** of the data.

$$
P(46 \leq X \leq 54) \approx 0.9544 \text{ or } 95.44\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Grouped Data Mean & Variance

**Problem:** Calculate the mean and standard deviation for the following frequency distribution.

| Class Interval | Frequency (f) |
|---|---|
| 10 – 20 | 5 |
| 20 – 30 | 10 |
| 30 – 40 | 15 |
| 40 – 50 | 10 |
| 50 – 60 | 5 |

### Step 1: Find midpoints (x) and compute fx.

| Interval | Midpoint (x) | f | f·x | f·x² |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10–20 | 15 | 5 | 75 | 1125 |
| 20–30 | 25 | 10 | 250 | 6250 |
| 30–40 | 35 | 15 | 525 | 18375 |
| 40–50 | 45 | 10 | 450 | 20250 |
| 50–60 | 55 | 5 | 275 | 15125 |
| **Total** | — | **45** | **1575** | **61125** |

### Step 2: Calculate the mean.

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f x}{\sum f} = \dfrac{1575}{45} = 35.0
$$

### Step 3: Calculate the variance.

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum f x^2}{\sum f} - \bar{x}^2 = \dfrac{61125}{45} - (35)^2
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = 1358.333 - 1225 = 133.333
$$

### Step 4: Calculate the standard deviation.

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{133.333} = 11.547
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Properties of the Normal Curve

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Symmetry** | Perfectly symmetric about the mean (μ) |
| **Mean = Median = Mode** | All three central measures coincide at the peak |
| **Bell Shape** | Unimodal, smooth, asymptotic to the X-axis |
| **Total Area** | Area under the curve = 1 (probability axiom) |
| **Inflection Points** | Located at μ ± σ (curvature changes) |
| **Skewness** | Zero (perfectly symmetric) |
| **Kurtosis** | Mesokurtic (kurtosis = 3) |
| **Asymptote** | The tails approach but never touch the X-axis |
| **Z-Transformation** | Any normal variable can be standardized to Z ~ N(0,1) |
| **Empirical Rule** | 68-95-99.7 within ±1σ, ±2σ, ±3σ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: List the Seven Tools of Quality and explain the purpose of the Pareto Chart in brief.**

**Model Answer:**

The Seven Tools of Quality, also known as **Ishikawa's 7QC Tools**, are:

1. Cause-and-Effect Diagram
2. Check Sheet
3. Control Chart
4. Histogram
5. Pareto Chart
6. Scatter Diagram
7. Flowchart

**Pareto Chart:** It is a graphical tool that combines a bar chart (with bars arranged in descending order of frequency) and a cumulative percentage line. It is based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, which states that roughly 80% of the problems are caused by 20% of the causes ("vital few vs. trivial many"). Its primary purpose is **prioritization** — helping quality teams focus efforts on the most significant problems first, thereby maximizing the impact of improvement initiatives.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between Population and Sample. Why is "n − 1" used in the denominator when calculating sample variance?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Population | Sample |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Entire group of interest | Subset of the population |
| **Size** | N (large, often infinite) | n (small, manageable) |
| **Parameters/Statistics** | Greek letters: μ, σ, σ² | Latin letters: $\bar{x}$, s, s² |
| **Data Availability** | Difficult/expensive to collect | Easier to collect |
| **Goal** | True characteristics of the whole | Estimate of population parameters |

**Reason for "n − 1" (Bessel's Correction):**
When calculating sample variance, using just "n" in the denominator produces a **biased estimator** that systematically underestimates the true population variance. The degrees of freedom for a sample of size n, after estimating the mean, is **(n − 1)**. Dividing by (n − 1) instead of n makes the sample variance an **unbiased estimator** of the population variance σ². As sample size grows large, the difference between n and (n − 1) becomes negligible.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q (a) (7 marks):** The marks obtained by 8 students in a class test are: 45, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80, 90. Calculate the mean, median, mode, range, variance, and standard deviation.

**Q (b) (7 marks):** A machine produces bolts with lengths normally distributed with mean μ = 10 cm and standard deviation σ = 0.5 cm. Find the probability that a randomly selected bolt has a length (i) between 9.0 cm and 11.0 cm, (ii) less than 9.5 cm.

---

### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Step 1: Sort the data** (already sorted): 45, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80, 90
n = 8

**Step 2: Calculate the Mean.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 45 + 55 + 60 + 65 + 70 + 75 + 80 + 90 = 540 \\
\bar{x} &= \dfrac{540}{8} = 67.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Median.**

Since n = 8 (even), median = average of 4th and 5th values.

$$
\text{Median} = \dfrac{x_{(4)} + x_{(5)}}{2} = \dfrac{65 + 70}{2} = 67.5
$$

**Step 4: Determine the Mode.**

No value repeats → all values occur once. **Dataset is multimodal/has no unique mode.** (KTU accepts: "No mode" or "All values are modes".)

**Step 5: Calculate the Range.**

$$
R = X_{\max} - X_{\min} = 90 - 45 = 45
$$

**Step 6: Calculate the Variance.**

| $x_i$ | $(x_i - 67.5)$ | $(x_i - 67.5)^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 45 | −22.5 | 506.25 |
| 55 | −12.5 | 156.25 |
| 60 | −7.5 | 56.25 |
| 65 | −2.5 | 6.25 |
| 70 | +2.5 | 6.25 |
| 75 | +7.5 | 56.25 |
| 80 | +12.5 | 156.25 |
| 90 | +22.5 | 506.25 |
| **Sum** | 0 | **1450** |

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1450}{8} = 181.25
$$

$$
s^2 = \dfrac{1450}{7} = 207.14 \text{ (sample variance)}
$$

**Step 7: Standard Deviation.**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{181.25} \approx 13.46
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{207.14} \approx 14.39 \text{ (sample std. dev.)}
$$

**Final Answer (a):** Mean = 67.5, Median = 67.5, Mode = None, Range = 45, σ² = 181.25, σ ≈ 13.46.

---

### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Given:** μ = 10 cm, σ = 0.5 cm

**(i) P(9.0 ≤ X ≤ 11.0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \dfrac{9.0 - 10}{0.5} = -2.0 \\
Z_2 &= \dfrac{11.0 - 10}{0.5} = +2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **Empirical Rule**, the interval [μ − 2σ, μ + 2σ] contains approximately **95.44%** of the observations.

$$
P(9.0 \leq X \leq 11.0) \approx 0.9544
$$

**(ii) P(X < 9.5):**

$$
Z = \dfrac{9.5 - 10}{0.5} = -1.0
$$

From the standard normal table, Φ(−1.0) = 0.1587.

$$
P(X < 9.5) = 0.1587 \text{ or } 15.87\%
$$

**Final Answer (b):** (i) 95.44%, (ii) 15.87%.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q (a) (7 marks):** Explain in detail the **Seven Tools of Quality** with a neat sketch of a Cause-and-Effect Diagram and its applications in manufacturing.

**Q (b) (7 marks):** The following data represents the lifetime (in hours) of 10 electric bulbs. Compute the mean, variance, and standard deviation. Data: 1050, 1100, 1080, 1120, 1090, 1070, 1110, 1060, 1130, 1090.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (a)

The **Seven Tools of Quality** (popularized by Kaoru Ishikawa) are visual and statistical aids used to identify, analyze, and solve quality-related problems.

1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Fishbone/Ishikawa Diagram):** Identifies the root causes of a problem. Categories: 6M (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature).
2. **Check Sheet:** A structured form for systematic data collection at the source.
3. **Control Chart (Shewhart Chart):** Monitors process stability over time using UCL, CL, and LCL.
4. **Histogram:** Bar graph showing frequency distribution of a dataset.
5. **Pareto Chart:** Bar + cumulative line chart for prioritization using 80/20 rule.
6. **Scatter Diagram:** Plot of two variables to identify correlation.
7. **Flowchart:** Pictorial representation of process steps.

**Cause-and-Effect Diagram — Sketch:**

```
                                  MATERIAL
                                      |
METHOD ----|                                    |---- MACHINE
           |          P R O B L E M           |
MEASUREMENT|       (HIGH DEFECT RATE)         |---- MANPOWER
           |                                    |
  ENVIRON--|                                    |---- (others)
MENT       

              (Effect)  ←—————  (Spine)  —————→  (Causes branching out)
```

**Applications in Manufacturing:**

- Identifying root causes of defects in a production line.
- Brainstorming in Kaizen events.
- Solving chronic issues in Six Sigma DMAIC (Analyze phase).
- Documenting standard operating procedures (SOPs) deviations.
- Training new engineers on process thinking.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Data:** 1050, 1100, 1080, 1120, 1090, 1070, 1110, 1060, 1130, 1090; n = 10

**Step 1: Sum of observations.**

$$
\sum x_i = 1050 + 1100 + 1080 + 1120 + 1090 + 1070 + 1110 + 1060 + 1130 + 1090 = 10900
$$

**Step 2: Mean.**

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{10900}{10} = 1090 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Compute deviations and squared deviations.**

| $x_i$ | $(x_i - 1090)$ | $(x_i - 1090)^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1050 | −40 | 1600 |
| 1100 | +10 | 100 |
| 1080 | −10 | 100 |
| 1120 | +30 | 900 |
| 1090 | 0 | 0 |
| 1070 | −20 | 400 |
| 1110 | +20 | 400 |
| 1060 | −30 | 900 |
| 1130 | +40 | 1600 |
| 1090 | 0 | 0 |
| **Sum** | 0 | **6000** |

**Step 4: Population Variance.**

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N} = \dfrac{6000}{10} = 600 \text{ hours}^2
$$

**Step 5: Population Standard Deviation.**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{600} = 24.495 \approx 24.49 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 6: Sample Variance & Std. Deviation (optional).**

$$
s^2 = \dfrac{6000}{9} = 666.67, \quad s = \sqrt{666.67} = 25.82
$$

**Final Answer (b):** Mean = 1090 hrs, σ² = 600, σ ≈ 24.49 hrs.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing n and (n − 1):** Using n in the denominator for sample variance. Always use **(n − 1)** for sample variance (Bessel's correction) unless the question explicitly asks for population variance.
> 2. **Median Calculation Error:** Forgetting to **sort the data first** before finding the middle value. For even n, the median is the **average of the two middle values** — not the lower or upper of the two.
> 3. **No Mode Declaration:** When no value repeats, students often write a random value. Correct answer: **"No unique mode"** or **"All values are modes"**.
> 4. **Sign Errors in Deviations:** $(x_i - \mu)$ — subtract the mean from each value; do not reverse.
> 5. **Z-Score Direction:** $Z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ — numerator order matters; reversed sign flips the entire interpretation.
> 6. **Empirical Rule Misapplication:** The 68-95-99.7 rule applies **only** to normal distributions, not to any bell-shaped curve.
> 7. **Not Showing Working:** KTU examiners award **step marks**. Always show the table of $(x_i - \bar{x})$ and $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$, even if the question is short.
> 8. **Seven Tools Confusion:** Mistaking Scatter Diagram (correlation) with Histogram (distribution). They serve different purposes.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 2**

### 🔹 Seven Tools of Quality (Mnemonic: **"CCH-PSCF"**)
- **C**ause-and-Effect (Fishbone) → Root causes
- **C**heck Sheet → Data collection
- **C**ontrol Chart → Process monitoring
- **H**istogram → Frequency distribution
- **P**areto Chart → 80/20 prioritization
- **S**catter Diagram → Correlation
- **F**lowchart → Process mapping

### 🔹 Central Tendency
- Mean = $\sum x_i / n$ (sensitive to outliers)
- Median = middle value (robust to outliers)
- Mode = most frequent (use for categorical data)
- For symmetric data: Mean = Median = Mode
- For right-skewed: Mean > Median > Mode
- For left-skewed: Mean < Median < Mode

### 🔹 Dispersion
- Range = Max − Min (crude, sensitive to outliers)
- Variance = average squared deviation
- Std. Deviation = √Variance (in same units as data)
- Population: divide by **N**; Sample: divide by **(n − 1)**

### 🔹 Population vs. Sample
- Population: Greek letters (μ, σ)
- Sample: Latin letters ($\bar{x}$, s)
- Sample is used to **estimate** population parameters

### 🔹 Normal Curve
- Symmetric, bell-shaped, unimodal
- Mean = Median = Mode = μ
- Empirical Rule: 68% (±1σ), 95% (±2σ), 99.7% (±3σ)
- Z-score: $Z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$
- Total area under curve = 1
- Tails approach but never touch the X-axis

### 🔹 Key Formulas to Memorize
- $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$
- $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}$
- $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$
- $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$
- UCL = μ + 3σ, LCL = μ − 3σ

### 🔹 Exam Strategy
- Always **show working tables** for variance/std dev.
- Mention **units** (e.g., mm, kg, hours) in final answers.
- For Normal Curve problems, convert to **Z-scores first**, then refer to standard normal tables or the Empirical Rule.
- Draw **neat labeled diagrams** for Seven Tools questions (especially Fishbone, Control Chart, Pareto).
- In Part A, write **brief but precise** answers (3–4 lines per question).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Juran Trilogy** is a universal framework for managing quality in any organization, conceptualized by **Dr. Joseph M. Juran**, one of the founding fathers of modern quality management. It comprises three interrelated managerial processes collectively called the *Quality Trilogy*:

1. **Quality Planning (QP)** – Designing and establishing the processes, products, and services to meet customer needs.
2. **Quality Control (QC)** – Operating the processes and evaluating their performance against quality goals.
3. **Quality Improvement (QI)** – Establishing an infrastructure capable of continuously raising the quality standards to breakthrough levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT636 – Module 3):**
> The Juran Trilogy forms the conceptual backbone of *Continuous Process Improvement* under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Examiners frequently frame 14-mark questions asking students to **"Explain the Juran Trilogy with a neat diagram and discuss how it enables continuous improvement"** (Course Outcome **CO3** – Bloom's Level: *Understand* / *Apply*).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Juran Trilogy as a Road Trip**
>
> Imagine you are planning a long road trip in your car:
> - **Quality Planning** is the *pre-trip phase* — you decide the destination (customer needs), the route (process design), and what car to take (product features). You map the journey on paper.
> - **Quality Control** is the *driving phase* — you constantly glance at the GPS (actual performance) and compare it to your planned route (quality goals). If you deviate, you steer back (corrective action).
> - **Quality Improvement** is the *upgrading phase* — after the trip, you notice the engine is sluggish on hills. You fit a turbocharger (breakthrough improvement) so the next trip is faster, smoother, and more efficient.
>
> This loop repeats for every subsequent trip — a perfect depiction of **continuous improvement**.

In essence:
- **QP** asks: *"What do we need to do?"*
- **QC** asks: *"Are we doing it right?"*
- **QI** asks: *"How can we do it even better?"*

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Breakthrough**: A sudden, dramatic improvement in a specific area, often tied to Quantitative Financial Goals (QFG).
> - **Chronic Waste / Chronic Loss**: The invisible, ever-present inefficiencies that accumulate over time (e.g., the 85% of problems that are *system-controlled*, per Juran's **Pareto Principle (85/15 Rule)**).
> - **Pareto Principle (85/15 Rule)**: Juran adapted Pareto's law, observing that **85%** of an organization's quality problems are *system-controlled* (management's responsibility), while only **15%** are *worker-controlled*.
> - **Sponsor / Project Team / Facilitator**: Roles that operationalize the QI process.

## 1.4 Juran's Position Among Quality Gurus

| Guru | Core Philosophy | Key Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | Statistical Process Control & System Thinking | 14 Points for Management |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Managerial Approach; Quality = Fitness for Use | **Juran Trilogy** |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Quality is Free; Conformance to Requirements | Zero Defects, Quality Vaccine |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC) | Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars — Exhaustive Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 PILLAR 1: Quality Planning (QP)

**Definition:** The structured activity of developing products, services, and processes necessary to meet customer needs and generate customer satisfaction.

**Operational Logic Steps (The Juran Quality Planning Road Map):**

1. **Identify the Customers** — Distinguish *internal* customers (next process in the chain) and *external* customers (end users).
2. **Determine Customer Needs (Voice of Customer — VOC)** — Capture stated, real, perceived, and cultural needs.
3. **Develop Product Features** — Translate needs into measurable, designable product/service specifications.
4. **Develop Process Features** — Design the operational process capable of producing the required product features.
5. **Transfer the Plan to Operating Forces** — Hand over the plan via training, documentation, and resource allocation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why QP Matters:** Without proper planning, no amount of control or improvement can rescue a process designed for the wrong outcome. QP is *proactive*; QC and QI are *reactive* and *progressive* respectively.

### 2.1.2 PILLAR 2: Quality Control (QC)

**Definition:** The regulatory process of evaluating actual quality performance, comparing it with quality goals, and acting on the difference.

**Operational Logic Steps (The Juran Quality Control Loop — known as the *Shewhart Cycle* in spirit):**

1. **Evaluate Actual Quality Performance** — Collect real-time operational data (defect rates, yield, customer complaints).
2. **Compare Actual Performance with Quality Goals** — Measure the *deviation* from the planned benchmark.
3. **Act on the Difference** — If deviation is adverse, apply corrective action; if favourable, document and stabilize.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Quality Control is **in-process and self-sustaining**. It maintains the *status quo of an established standard*. It does **not** raise the standard — that is QI's job.

### 2.1.3 PILLAR 3: Quality Improvement (QI)

**Definition:** The infrastructure and methodology for creating *breakthrough* improvements to achieve unprecedented levels of performance.

**Operational Logic Steps (The Juran Improvement Sequence — also called the *Breakthrough Sequence*):**

1. **Establish the Infrastructure** — Appoint sponsors, project teams, and facilitators.
2. **Identify Improvement Projects** — Use the *Pareto Principle* to find the *vital few* problems causing the *trivial many* losses.
3. **Establish Project Teams** — Assign responsibility with clear authority.
4. **Provide Resources, Motivation, and Training** — Equip teams to diagnose and act.
5. **Diagnose Causes** — Apply statistical and analytical tools (Pareto charts, cause-and-effect diagrams).
6. **Establish Remedies** — Design and pilot solutions.
7. **Establish Controls** — Lock in gains by standardizing the new process.
8. **Demonstrate Improvement** — Quantify the gain in financial / quality terms.
9. **Propagate the Improvement** — Replicate successful improvements across the organization.

## 2.2 The Juran Trilogy — Interconnection Logic

The trilogy is **not linear** — it forms a **closed-loop spiral**:

$$QP \longrightarrow QC \longrightarrow QI \longrightarrow (back\ to\ a\ higher\ level\ of\ QP)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - QP creates the *goals*.
> - QC maintains the *goals*.
> - QI raises the *goals* to a new breakthrough level.
> - This triggers a **new round of QP** at the elevated benchmark.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Quality Planning (QP)** | **Quality Control (QC)** | **Quality Improvement (QI)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core Question | What do we need to do? | Are we doing it right? | How can we do it even better? |
| Phase of Activity | Pre-emptive (Before) | Concurrent (During) | Progressive (After) |
| Key Output | Quality Goals, Specifications | Stable Process | Breakthrough Performance |
| Nature of Change | Establishing standards | Holding the gain | Raising the standard |
| Tools Used | Quality Function Deployment (QFD), Market Research | Control Charts, Inspection | Pareto Analysis, Statistical Tools, PDCA |
| Output Metric | Specification documents | Defect rate, yield | Cost reduction, % improvement |
| KTU Bloom's Level | Remember, Understand | Apply, Analyze | Analyze, Evaluate, Create |

## 2.4 The 85/15 Rule (Pareto Principle in Quality)

Juran observed in Western organizations that:

$$\approx 85\%\ of\ defects \longrightarrow Caused\ by\ System\ (Management)$$

$$\approx 15\%\ of\ defects \longrightarrow Caused\ by\ Workers$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Implication:** Top management is responsible for the *root causes* of most quality failures. This shifts the focus from blaming workers to redesigning systems — a central tenet of TQM.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Sequencing new product launches (QP), monitoring production line SPC (QC), launching Kaizen projects (QI).
- **Software Engineering:** Requirements elicitation (QP), unit testing (QC), Agile retrospectives (QI).
- **Service Industry:** Designing SOPs (QP), auditing service metrics (QC), Six Sigma DMAIC projects (QI).
- **Civil/Mechanical Projects:** Quality plans per ISO 9001, on-site inspections, post-completion review workshops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Logical Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Foundation of the Improvement Spiral

While the Juran Trilogy is managerial, the **gain achieved in each QI cycle** can be quantified. Let us derive the **cumulative improvement factor** when the trilogy is applied iteratively.

### 3.1.1 Defining the Variables

Let:
- $P_0$ = Initial process performance baseline (e.g., defect rate, cycle time, cost).
- $g$ = Fractional gain achieved in **one** QI cycle (e.g., $g = 0.20$ for a 20% improvement).
- $n$ = Number of continuous QI cycles executed.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Cumulative Performance

**Step 1:** Initial performance level is the baseline.

$$P_0 = P_0$$

**Step 2:** After Cycle 1, performance is *reduced* (for defect-related metrics) by the factor $(1 - g)$.

$$P_1 = P_0 \times (1 - g)$$

**Step 3:** After Cycle 2, a new QP is established at the *new* baseline $P_1$, then QI is applied again.

$$P_2 = P_1 \times (1 - g) = P_0 \times (1 - g)^2$$

**Step 4:** By mathematical induction, after $n$ cycles:

$$P_n = P_0 \times (1 - g)^n$$

**Step 5:** The **cumulative fractional improvement** (gain in performance) after $n$ cycles is:

$$\text{Total Improvement} = \frac{P_0 - P_n}{P_0} = 1 - (1 - g)^n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example:**
> Let $P_0 = 100$ defects/day, $g = 0.15$ (15% reduction per QI cycle).
> After $n = 5$ cycles:

$$P_5 = 100 \times (1 - 0.15)^5 = 100 \times (0.85)^5$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.85)^1 &= 0.8500 \\
(0.85)^2 &= 0.7225 \\
(0.85)^3 &= 0.6141 \\
(0.85)^4 &= 0.5220 \\
(0.85)^5 &= 0.4437 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$P_5 = 100 \times 0.4437 = 44.37\ defects/day$$

$$\text{Total Improvement} = 1 - 0.4437 = 0.5563 = 55.63\%$$

**Interpretation:** Five QI cycles of 15% each yield a **~55.6% cumulative reduction** in defects. This non-linear, *exponential* decay is the mathematical proof of why **continuous improvement (kaizen) compounds over time** — a frequent 14-mark question in KTU ESE.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the Trilogy to KTU Course Outcomes

| **KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO)** | **Trilogy Component Mapped** | **Bloom's Cognitive Level** | **Assessment Weightage (Typical)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO3: Apply TQM tools to real-world quality problems | QI (Pareto, SPC) | Apply / Analyze | 60% |
| CO2: Explain the philosophy of TQM gurus | QP + QC + QI overview | Understand | 25% |
| CO4: Develop quality improvement strategies | QI (Breakthrough sequence) | Create / Evaluate | 15% |

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Juran vs. Deming vs. Crosby

| **Parameter** | **Juran Trilogy** | **Deming Cycle (PDCA)** | **Crosby's Zero Defects** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Focus | Managerial Processes | Statistical / System Thinking | Conformance to Requirements |
| Number of Steps | 3 (Trilogy) | 4 (Plan-Do-Check-Act) | 4 absolutes of Quality |
| Stresses | Customer Needs + Improvement | Variation Reduction | Doing it right the first time |
| Improvement Type | Breakthrough (Big Bang) + Kaizen | Continuous, Iterative | Cultural / Behavioural |
| Source of Problems | 85% System (Management) | 85% System (Management) | 100% Prevention-based |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The PDCA cycle of Deming operates *within* Quality Control of the Juran Trilogy. This is a high-yield comparison question.

## 3.4 The Role of Senior Management in the Trilogy

Juran explicitly identified the **Upper Management's** responsibilities:

1. **Establish quality goals** (QP contribution).
2. **Provide resources** for quality control and improvement.
3. **Conduct periodic reviews** of the quality system.
4. **Recognize and reward** improvement efforts.

This alignment with **ISO 9001 Clause 5 (Leadership)** makes the Juran Trilogy directly applicable to certified quality management systems in engineering firms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Juran Trilogy — Master Cycle Diagram


## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Trilogy in an Industrial Plant

```mermaid

    subgraph ManagementLayer["Management Layer Strategic Direction"]
```

> [!NOTE]

> - The **Management Layer** drives all three pillars.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the Juran Trilogy. Name its three components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Juran Trilogy, formulated by Dr. Joseph M. Juran, is a universal managerial framework for managing quality across any organization. It consists of three interrelated processes:

1. **Quality Planning (QP):** Designing products, services, and processes to meet customer needs.
2. **Quality Control (QC):** Evaluating actual performance against quality goals and acting on deviations.
3. **Quality Improvement (QI):** Establishing infrastructure to achieve breakthrough, ongoing improvements in quality performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Pattern:** 1 mark for the definition + 1 mark each for any two components (or 0.5 + 0.5 + 1 if all three listed briefly).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] State Juran's 85/15 Rule and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Juran's **85/15 Rule** (adapted from the Pareto Principle) states that approximately **85%** of an organization's quality problems are caused by **systems and processes** (i.e., are within the control of management), while only **15%** are caused by **workers**.

**Significance (2 marks):**
- It shifts the focus from blaming frontline workers to redesigning the underlying system.
- It justifies management's central role in TQM and provides the rationale for the trilogy's *improvement* component.
- It implies that fixing the system will automatically fix the worker's environment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern:** You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU Model Question – Continuous Improvement Theme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the three processes of the Juran Trilogy in detail. Draw a neat block diagram showing their interrelation.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Discuss how the Juran Trilogy enables continuous process improvement in a manufacturing industry. Highlight the role of the 85/15 Rule.*

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) The Three Processes of the Juran Trilogy (7 marks):**

1. **Quality Planning (QP) — 2.5 marks:**
   - Customer identification → customer needs discovery → product design → process design → transfer to operations.
   - The goal of QP is to *establish* the targets and the means to reach them.
2. **Quality Control (QC) — 2.5 marks:**
   - Evaluate actual performance → compare with goals → act on the deviation.
   - QC operates the *status quo* and ensures stability of the planned process.
3. **Quality Improvement (QI) — 2 marks:**
   - Infrastructure setup → project identification (Pareto) → team formation → diagnosis → remedy → control → propagation.
   - QI aims at *breakthrough* — a step change in performance.

**Interrelation Diagram (to be drawn in answer script — 1 mark):**

```
   QP ── Goals ──▶ QC ── Stable Process ──▶ QI ── Breakthrough ──▶ New QP (higher level)
                          ▲                                            │
                          └──────────── Feedback ─────────────────────┘
```

**(b) Continuous Improvement in Manufacturing (7 marks):**

- **The Improvement Spiral:** Every QI cycle elevates the benchmark, which triggers a *new* QP cycle, and the process repeats. This creates the *spiral of continuous improvement*.
- **Project-Based Improvement:** Manufacturing units use Six Sigma DMAIC and Kaizen workshops as practical implementations of QI.
- **Role of the 85/15 Rule:** Since 85% of defects are *system-induced*, management invests in *system-level* redesigns (workflow, tooling, automation). Worker-level interventions are reserved for the remaining 15%.
- **Example:** A switchgear manufacturing plant reduces its rejection rate from 8% to 2% over four QI cycles; the process capability index ($C_p$) improves correspondingly, validating continuous improvement mathematically.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**
**[KTU Model Question – Application & Comparison Theme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe the nine-step Juran Quality Improvement (Breakthrough) Sequence. Why is it called a "Breakthrough" approach?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare the Juran Trilogy with the Deming PDCA Cycle. Can the two frameworks coexist in a single organization? Justify.*

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) The Nine-Step Breakthrough Sequence (7 marks — 0.5 mark per step + 2.5 marks for explanation):**

| Step | Activity | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Establish infrastructure | Sponsors, teams, budget |
| 2 | Identify improvement projects | Pareto — vital few |
| 3 | Establish project teams | Multi-functional teams |
| 4 | Provide resources, motivation, training | Capability building |
| 5 | Diagnose causes | Statistical diagnosis |
| 6 | Establish remedies | Design solutions |
| 7 | Establish controls | Hold the gain |
| 8 | Demonstrate improvement | Financial justification |
| 9 | Propagate improvement | Replicate to all areas |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Breakthrough"?** (2.5 marks)
> The sequence is *project-based* with dedicated teams, resources, and timelines — leading to a *sudden, dramatic, sustainable* performance leap, distinct from incremental day-to-day improvement. Juran called it *breakthrough* because it breaks through the *chronic waste* layer that ordinary QC cannot remove.

**(b) Juran Trilogy vs. Deming PDCA (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Juran Trilogy | Deming PDCA |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Three managerial processes | Single iterative cycle |
| Focus | Customer-driven, project-based | Variation reduction, system-wide |
| Tools | Pareto, Cost of Quality, Quality Planning | SPC, Shewhart Charts, Hypothesis Testing |
| Improvement Type | Breakthrough + Kaizen | Continuous, incremental |

**Coexistence Justification (3 marks):**
- The two frameworks are **complementary**, not competing.
- **Deming's PDCA** can be used *within* the Quality Control pillar of the Juran Trilogy to maintain stability.
- **Juran's QI projects** can use **Deming's statistical tools** (SPC, control charts) for diagnosis and verification.
- Modern TQM implementations in firms like **Toyota, GE, and Bosch** use both — Juran for the strategic roadmap and Deming for the tactical statistical engine.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating QC with QI:** Many students write that QC "improves" quality. Mark cut: -2. QC *maintains*; QI *improves*. The examiner's key explicitly tests this distinction.
> 2. **Missing the 85/15 Rule in QP/QC questions:** Even if the question is on QP alone, citing the 85/15 Rule shows depth and earns +1 to +2 marks as discretionary marks.
> 3. **Linear Diagram Instead of Cyclic Diagram:** The trilogy is a **closed-loop spiral**, not a one-way flow. Drawing straight arrows will lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the Pareto Principle in QI:** The Breakthrough Sequence **must** mention Pareto (Step 2) for full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the Senior Management Role:** Juran explicitly assigns *upper management* the responsibility of establishing infrastructure. A 14-mark answer without this is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- The **Juran Trilogy** = **Quality Planning + Quality Control + Quality Improvement**.
- **Joseph M. Juran** coined the term *Fitness for Use* and the *85/15 Rule*.
- **QP** is **proactive**; **QC** is **concurrent (maintains)**; **QI** is **progressive (improves)**.
- The trilogy forms a **closed-loop spiral**, not a linear flow.
- The **Breakthrough Sequence** has **9 steps**, beginning with infrastructure and ending with propagation.
- The **85/15 Rule** means **management** owns most quality problems, not workers.
- **Juran vs. Deming:** Trilogy is managerial; PDCA is operational. They **complement** each other.
- **Juran vs. Crosby:** Trilogy is systematic; Crosby's is *behavioural/preventive* (Zero Defects).
- **KTU Hot Spot:** Examiner almost always asks for a **neat diagram** — practice drawing the cyclic flow.
- **Course Outcomes Tested:** CO3 (Apply TQM tools), CO2 (Explain philosophy), CO4 (Develop strategies).
- **Key Tools to Mention in Answers:** Pareto chart, Control charts, Cost of Quality, QFD, Cause-and-Effect diagram.
- **Real-World Mapping:** ISO 9001 QMS, Six Sigma DMAIC, Lean Manufacturing, Kaizen — all derive from the trilogy.
- **Mnemonic for the Trilogy:** **"P-C-I"** = **Plan – Control – Improve**.
- **Mnemonic for the QI Sequence:** **"I-I-R-D-R-C-D-P"** = **Infrastructure, Identify, Resources, Diagnose, Remedies, Controls, Demonstrate, Propagate**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PDSA Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PDSA Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) Cycle** is a systematic, iterative, four-stage quality improvement framework used to test changes in real-world settings by planning a change, trying it, observing the results, and acting on what is learned. In the KTU 2024 TQM syllabus, the PDSA cycle is positioned as the **foundational scientific method of continuous improvement**, forming the operational engine of Kaizen and the broader Deming philosophy of management.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The PDSA cycle is sometimes referred to as the **Deming Wheel**, **Deming Cycle**, or **Shewhart Cycle**. It is the *theoretical engine* under which tools like the 5 Whys, Pareto Analysis, Cause-and-Effect Diagrams, and Control Charts operate.

The cycle is formally expressed as:

$$
\boxed{\text{PDSA} \equiv \{\,\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Study} \rightarrow \text{Act}\,\}_{\text{iterative}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** The PDSA cycle is fundamentally a *hypothesis-testing loop*. Every rotation generates empirical data that either validates, refines, or rejects the current improvement hypothesis. The iteration count $N \geq 1$ depends on the convergence of measured outcomes toward the target Key Performance Indicator (KPI).

## 1.2 Historical Lineage (Origin & Evolution)

| Era | Contributor | Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **1920s** | **Walter A. Shewhart** | Originated the iterative learning cycle in Bell Labs |
| **1950s** | **W. Edwards Deming** | Popularized and renamed the cycle (PDSA) in post-WWII Japan |
| **1980s** | **Kaizen Institute** | Integrated PDSA into continuous improvement systems globally |
| **2000s** | **Institute for Healthcare Improvement (IHI)** | Standardized PDSA in healthcare and education |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The GPS Recalibration Loop

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are driving from your home in Kerala to a hill station like Munnar using Google Maps.
> - **PLAN:** You set the destination and study the suggested route.
> - **DO:** You start driving along the route.
> - **STUDY:** You monitor your actual position, time taken, and traffic congestion.
> - **ACT:** If the route is blocked, you accept the rerouting suggestion (and *standardize* it if it is faster) or return to the previous route.
>
> The GPS *never* stops recalibrating; it loops continuously. PDSA behaves identically — it is the **GPS of organizational improvement**.

## 1.4 Why PDSA? — The Logical Justification

Most organizational changes fail because they are:
1. Implemented at full scale without pilot testing.
2. Never scientifically evaluated post-implementation.
3. One-time events with no iterative learning mechanism.

PDSA solves all three through its **built-in test-and-learn architecture**. It is explicitly demanded by the KTU 2024 TQM syllabus as the operational mechanism for executing the **Deming Philosophy (System, Variation, Theory of Knowledge, Psychology)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Heuristic:** When an exam question uses the word *"systematically"*, *"iteratively"*, or *"scientifically test a change"*, it is *always* signalling the **PDSA cycle** as the expected answer framework.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Phases — Operational Logic Breakdown

The PDSA cycle is not a checklist; it is a **recursive learning engine**. Each phase has a specific cognitive role mapped directly to the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (RBT)** cognitive levels mandated by the KTU 2024 OBE framework.

### 2.1.1 Phase 1 — PLAN (RBT Level: Apply / Analyze)

The planning phase converts a problem into a **falsifiable hypothesis**. It is the most documentation-intensive phase.

**Core Activities:**
- Define the problem statement using the **5W2H** framework.
- Identify the root cause via tools: *Pareto Chart, Fishbone Diagram, 5 Whys, Scatter Diagram*.
- Formulate a measurable objective with a **SMART** target.
- Predict expected outcomes and select Key Performance Indicators (KPIs).
- Design the intervention and define the pilot scope, duration, and resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **SMART Objectives** (mandated by KTU module outcomes): **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-bound.

### 2.1.2 Phase 2 — DO (RBT Level: Apply)

The execution phase carries out the plan on a **small, controlled scale** to limit risk.

**Core Activities:**
- Document the process baseline before intervention.
- Execute the pilot with rigorous data collection.
- Log anomalies, deviations, and unexpected observations.
- Train the team on data-collection protocols.
- Capture both quantitative and qualitative feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** The "Do" phase is *not* full-scale implementation. KTU examiners explicitly penalize answers that confuse pilot testing with enterprise-wide rollout.

### 2.1.3 Phase 3 — STUDY (RBT Level: Evaluate / Analyze)

The analytical phase converts raw observations into **evidence-based conclusions**.

**Core Activities:**
- Compare observed results against the predicted KPIs.
- Use **Control Charts** to distinguish common-cause vs special-cause variation.
- Quantify the effect using **Process Capability Indices** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$).
- Identify gaps, side-effects, and confounding variables.
- Validate or reject the original hypothesis.

### 2.1.4 Phase 4 — ACT (RBT Level: Create / Evaluate)

The consolidation phase locks in the learning and propagates it.

**Three Possible Decisions in the ACT Phase:**

$$
\text{ACT Decision} =
\begin{cases}
\text{ADOPT} & \text{if hypothesis validated} \rightarrow \text{standardize, train, scale up} \\
\text{ADAPT} & \text{if partially validated} \rightarrow \text{modify plan, run new PDSA cycle} \\
\text{ABANDON} & \text{if hypothesis rejected} \rightarrow \text{redefine problem, restart cycle}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students terminate the cycle after the "Act" phase. KTU examiners specifically reward answers that emphasize the *recursion* — the next cycle begins with new knowledge from the previous one.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Tool Cheat Sheet

The table below is a **board-examination-ready reference** for the PDSA-related formulas, metrics, and tools a student is expected to recall and apply.

| Element | Formula / Tool | Application in PDSA | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Used in STUDY to measure inherent process capability | dimensionless |
| **Process Capability Index (shifted)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Used in STUDY to measure actual capability after centering | dimensionless |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$ | STUDY-phase defect quantification | defects per million |
| **Sigma Level Conversion** | $\sigma \approx \dfrac{1}{C_{pk}} \times 1.5 + 1.5$ | Maps capability to Six Sigma scale | $\sigma$ |
| **Pareto 80/20 Rule** | Cumulative \% defects vs. cumulative \% causes | PLAN-phase root-cause prioritization | percentage |
| **5W2H Framework** | What, Why, Where, When, Who, How, How much | PLAN-phase problem framing | qualitative |
| **Control Chart Limits** | $\text{UCL} = \bar{X} + 3\sigma \quad ; \quad \text{LCL} = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$ | STUDY-phase variation classification | variable-dependent |
| **Cycle Time Reduction** | $\Delta T = T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}}$ | DO/STUDY comparative metric | time unit |
| **First-Time-Right Rate** | $\text{FTR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{passed first time}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | DO-phase execution quality | percent |
| **Yield Rate** | $Y = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \cdots \times Y_n$ | STUDY-phase rolled throughput yield | percent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = standard deviation, USL/LSL = upper/lower specification limits, $D$ = defects, $N$ = units, $O$ = opportunities per unit.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | PDSA Application |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Reducing line rejection rate from **8% to 2%** in an automotive plant |
| **Software (KTU CS Branch)** | Iterative sprint-level quality improvement in Agile/Scrum |
| **Healthcare** | Reducing hospital-acquired infections through hand-hygiene pilots |
| **Civil Engineering** | Optimizing concrete mix-design curing time |
| **Education (NEP 2020)** | Improving student learning outcomes at the classroom level |

## 2.4 PDSA vs PDCA — The KTU-Favoured Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** Many textbooks (especially older ones) call this the PDCA cycle. Modern Deming literature prefers **PDSA** because "Study" implies rigorous analysis, while "Check" implies mere verification.

| Dimension | PDSA (Deming's preferred) | PDCA (older usage) |
|---|---|---|
| Originator's intent | Deep analytical study | Surface-level verification |
| Knowledge outcome | Theory-building | Status confirmation |
| KTU 2024 syllabus term | **PDSA** | Not preferred |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Decision Matrix

This section provides an **exhaustive, line-by-line analytical derivation** of how the PDSA cycle is operationalized in a real TQM project. Each phase is decomposed into its constituent sub-steps, decision rules, and quantitative checkpoints. The framework is intentionally over-specified because KTU 14-mark questions routinely demand this level of granularity.

## 3.1 The 5W2H Decomposition (Mandatory in PLAN Phase)

The PLAN phase cannot begin without a fully resolved 5W2H grid. Below is the explicit derivation of each element.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PLAN} &\equiv \Big\{\, W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4, W_5, H_1, H_2 \,\Big\} \\[4pt]
W_1 &: \text{What is the problem?} \rightarrow \text{Define defect } D \\
W_2 &: \text{Why is it occurring?} \rightarrow \text{Root cause via 5 Whys} \\
W_3 &: \text{Where is it happening?} \rightarrow \text{Identify process, location, machine} \\
W_4 &: \text{When does it occur?} \rightarrow \text{Time, shift, frequency mapping} \\
W_5 &: \text{Who is involved?} \rightarrow \text{Operator, supervisor, supplier} \\
H_1 &: \text{How will we intervene?} \rightarrow \text{Method, tool, procedure} \\
H_2 &: \text{How much will it cost?} \rightarrow \text{Capital, training, downtime}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This decomposition is isomorphic to a *requirements specification* in software engineering. Each $W_i$ and $H_j$ becomes a *testable acceptance criterion* for the subsequent DO phase.

## 3.2 Hypothesis Construction in PLAN

A PDSA-acceptable change must be a *falsifiable hypothesis* of the form:

$$
H_0 : \text{Intervention } I \text{ will not change } \text{KPI} \\
H_1 : \text{Intervention } I \text{ will change } \text{KPI by at least } \delta
$$

The measurable objective is therefore:

$$
\Delta \text{KPI} = \text{KPI}_{\text{after}} - \text{KPI}_{\text{before}} \geq \delta
$$

where $\delta$ is the **Minimum Practically Significant Difference (MPSD)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Heuristic:** A well-formed PDSA plan must specify a *numeric* $\delta$. Vague objectives like "improve quality" are penalized.

## 3.3 The DO Phase — Data Collection Protocol (Exhaustive)

A KTU full-mark answer for the DO phase must explicitly enumerate the following six activities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DO} \rightarrow \{ &\text{(1) Document baseline}, \\
&\text{(2) Train data collectors}, \\
&\text{(3) Execute pilot on } n \geq 30 \text{ samples}, \\
&\text{(4) Record } X_{\text{before}}, X_{\text{after}} \text{ for each KPI}, \\
&\text{(5) Capture process anomalies}, \\
&\text{(6) Store raw data in a control log} \,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $n \geq 30$?** The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) guarantees approximate normality of the sample mean, which is the prerequisite for using the $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and $\bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$ control limits in the STUDY phase.

## 3.4 The STUDY Phase — Statistical Decision Algorithm

The STUDY phase applies a **sequential decision algorithm** that converts raw observations into a verdict. The full logical flow is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compute } \bar{X}_{\text{after}}, s_{\text{after}} \text{ (sample mean and std. dev.)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compute } C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\bar{X}_{\text{after}} - \text{LSL}}{3 s_{\text{after}}}, \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}_{\text{after}}}{3 s_{\text{after}}}\right) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Compute } C_{pk}^{\text{before}} \text{ from baseline data} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Compute } \Delta C_{pk} = C_{pk}^{\text{after}} - C_{pk}^{\text{before}} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{If } \Delta C_{pk} \geq 0.33 \text{ AND } C_{pk}^{\text{after}} \geq 1.00 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ADOPT} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{If } 0 < \Delta C_{pk} < 0.33 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ADAPT (new PDSA)} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{If } \Delta C_{pk} \leq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ABANDON (redefine problem)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Threshold Rationale:** The threshold of $\Delta C_{pk} = 0.33$ corresponds to the industry-standard *minimum practically significant improvement* (one-third of a sigma level), aligning with Six Sigma DMAIC practice.

## 3.5 The ACT Phase — Standardization Mechanics

When the verdict is **ADOPT**, the organization must perform the following *lock-in* actions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACT-ADOPT} \rightarrow \{ &\text{(1) Update Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)}, \\
&\text{(2) Train all stakeholders (not just pilot team)}, \\
&\text{(3) Update FMEA and control plan}, \\
&\text{(4) Add new control chart to SPC system}, \\
&\text{(5) Begin monitoring the new baseline} \,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

When the verdict is **ADAPT**, the cycle restarts with modified $H_1$. The recursion can be modelled as:

$$
\text{PDSA}_{k+1} = f(\text{PDSA}_k, \text{Lessons Learned}_k)
$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the *knowledge-integration function* that uses the previous cycle's STUDY output to refine the next PLAN.

## 3.6 Quantitative Worked-Example Framework (Numerical Demo)

Consider a KTU-style numerical question: *"A manufacturing line produces shafts with diameter specification $20.00 \pm 0.10$ mm. The pre-PDSA sample of $n = 30$ gave $\bar{X}_1 = 20.04$ mm, $s_1 = 0.025$ mm. After the DO phase, $n = 30$ gave $\bar{X}_2 = 20.01$ mm, $s_2 = 0.020$ mm. Evaluate the PDSA STUDY verdict."*

**Step 1 — Identify limits:** USL $= 20.10$ mm, LSL $= 19.90$ mm.

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ before:**

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{before}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{20.04 - 19.90}{3(0.025)}, \dfrac{20.10 - 20.04}{3(0.025)}\right) = \min(1.867, 0.800) = 0.800
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$ after:**

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{20.01 - 19.90}{3(0.020)}, \dfrac{20.10 - 20.01}{3(0.020)}\right) = \min(1.833, 1.500) = 1.500
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta C_{pk}$:**

$$
\Delta C_{pk} = 1.500 - 0.800 = 0.700
$$

**Step 5 — Apply decision rule:** $\Delta C_{pk} = 0.700 \geq 0.33$ **and** $C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = 1.500 \geq 1.00$. **Verdict: ADOPT.**

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** The process *also* moved closer to the target (centring improved from $20.04$ to $20.01$), which is a *bonus* benefit of the intervention.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Primary Mermaid Diagram — The PDSA Cycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Organizational Problem Identified]) --> planA[PLAN Phase]
    planA --> planB[Define Objective SMART]
    planB --> planC[5W2H Analysis]
    planC --> planD[Formulate Hypothesis H1]
    planD --> planE[Select KPIs and MPSD]
    planE --> doA[DO Phase]
    doA --> doB[Train Pilot Team]
    doB --> doC[Collect Baseline Data]
    doC --> doD[Execute Pilot on n greater than 30]
    doD --> doE[Log Anomalies]
    doE --> studyA[STUDY Phase]
    studyA --> studyB[Compute Cpk Before and After]
    studyB --> studyC[Apply Control Charts]
    studyC --> studyD[Compare delta KPI to MPSD]
    studyD --> studyE{Decision Rule}
    studyE -->|Adopt| actA[ACT Adopt]
    studyE -->|Adapt| actB[ACT Adapt Restart PDSA]
    studyE -->|Abandon| actC[ACT Abandon Redefine]
    actA --> std1[Update SOP and FMEA]
    std1 --> std2[Train All Stakeholders]
    std2 --> std3[Rollout to Full Scale]
    std3 --> nextCycle1[Begin New PDSA Cycle]
    actB --> planA
    actC --> planA
    nextCycle1 --> planA
```

## 4.2 Secondary Mermaid Diagram — Tool–Phase Mapping Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLAN_TOOLS
        tool1[Pareto Chart]
        tool2[Fishbone Diagram]
        tool3[5 Whys]
        tool4[5W2H Grid]
        tool5[Affinity Diagram]
    end
    subgraph DO_TOOLS
        tool6[Check Sheets]
        tool7[Gantt Chart]
        tool8[Pilot Run Log]
        tool9[Histogram Baseline]
    end
    subgraph STUDY_TOOLS
        tool10[Control Chart Xbar R]
        tool11[Scatter Diagram]
        tool12[Capability Analysis]
        tool13[Hypothesis Test t Test]
    end
    subgraph ACT_TOOLS
        tool14[Standardization Matrix]
        tool15[FMEA Update]
        tool16[SOP Revision]
        tool17[Training Plan]
    end
    PLAN_TOOLS --> DO_TOOLS
    DO_TOOLS --> STUDY_TOOLS
    STUDY_TOOLS --> ACT_TOOLS
    ACT_TOOLS --> PLAN_TOOLS
```

## 4.3 Tertiary Mermaid Diagram — Decision Logic of the ACT Phase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[STUDY Output delta Cpk and Cpk after] --> q1{Is delta Cpk greater than 0.33}
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is delta Cpk greater than 0}
    q1 -->|Yes| q3{Is Cpk after at least 1.00}
    q2 -->|No| abandon[ABANDON Restart with New Problem Statement]
    q2 -->|Yes| adapt[ADAPT Modify Plan and Start New PDSA]
    q3 -->|No| adapt
    q3 -->|Yes| adopt[ADOPT Standardize and Scale Up]
    adopt --> monitor[Add to SPC Monitoring System]
    monitor --> feedback[Initiate Next Improvement Cycle]
    adapt --> feedback
    abandon --> feedback
```
