# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

*"Differentiate between the PDSA cycle and PDCA cycle. Which one is preferred in the modern Deming philosophy and why?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle was the original formulation popularised in earlier quality literature, where the third phase "Check" implies a superficial verification against a standard. The **PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act)** cycle, preferred by W. Edwards Deming in his later writings, replaces "Check" with "Study" to emphasise a **deep, analytical, and theory-building** phase. The PDSA variant is preferred in the modern Deming philosophy because it captures the *System of Profound Knowledge* requirement of building a predictive theory of the process, not merely confirming a number. The KTU 2024 TQM syllabus adopts the **PDSA** terminology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Difference (1), Deming's reasoning (1), KTU preference (1).

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

*"List the four phases of the PDSA cycle and state the primary objective of the STUDY phase."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four phases of the PDSA cycle are **Plan, Do, Study, and Act**. The primary objective of the **STUDY** phase is to **analyse the data collected during the DO phase, compare the observed outcomes against the predicted KPIs, and determine whether the change hypothesis is validated, partially validated, or rejected.** This phase uses statistical tools like control charts, Pareto analysis, and process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) to draw evidence-based conclusions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Four phases (1), STUDY objective (2).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Section B questions are offered in *OR* (internal choice) format. The student must answer *either* Question B1 *or* Question B2. Each question carries 7 marks per sub-part.

---

### **Question B1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain in detail the four phases of the **PDSA cycle** with a suitable real-world example. Discuss the role of **statistical process control** tools in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *"Continuous improvement is impossible without an iterative learning mechanism."* Critically evaluate this statement in the context of the **PDSA cycle** and the **Deming philosophy of management**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer B1(a) — 7 marks

The PDSA cycle consists of four iterative phases:

**1. PLAN Phase (2 marks):**
In this phase, the team identifies the problem, sets a SMART objective, predicts outcomes, and designs a data-collection plan. A real-world example is a hospital aiming to reduce patient-waiting time from 45 minutes to under 20 minutes in the OPD. Tools used here include the 5W2H grid, Pareto chart, fishbone diagram, and 5-Whys analysis.

**2. DO Phase (1.5 marks):**
The plan is executed on a small pilot scale (e.g., one OPD wing for two weeks). The team collects baseline data and pilot data using check sheets and run charts. Anomalies and unexpected observations are logged.

**3. STUDY Phase (2 marks):**
The data is analysed. The waiting-time mean drops from 45 to 22 minutes, and the standard deviation reduces. A control chart shows special-cause variation eliminated. The hypothesis is *partially* validated — improvement is real but the target (20 min) is not fully met. A new PDSA cycle is required.

**4. ACT Phase (1.5 marks):**
Because the result is *partially* successful, the team chooses to **ADAPT** — it adopts the new triage process, modifies the appointment slot duration, and re-enters PLAN with a refined hypothesis. The standardisation is partial, and the cycle restarts.

**Role of SPC Tools:** In PLAN, SPC helps define realistic process capability targets. In DO, run charts and check sheets capture data. In STUDY, control charts (X-bar, R, p-charts) and $C_{pk}$ analysis distinguish common-cause and special-cause variation. In ACT, the validated control limits become the new operational baseline for the SPC system.

---

#### Model Answer B1(b) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The statement is a foundational principle of the Deming philosophy. Continuous improvement (Kaizen) requires *learning*, and learning requires *iteration*. The PDSA cycle is the structural embodiment of this principle.

**Argument in Favour (3 marks):**
1. **Knowledge Building:** Every PDSA rotation converts tacit assumptions into explicit, testable knowledge. Without iteration, organizations operate on hearsay.
2. **Reduction of Resistance to Change:** Small iterative cycles lower psychological resistance compared to big-bang transformations.
3. **Adaptation to Variation:** Real systems exhibit common-cause and special-cause variation. Only iterative testing reveals which variation is present.
4. **Statistical Rigor:** A single change cycle provides one data point; multiple cycles allow trend analysis and process-capability tracking.

**Counter-Argument & Nuance (1.5 marks):**
1. PDSA assumes management's commitment to a *learning organisation*; without it, the cycle becomes a bureaucratic ritual.
2. PDSA may be slow for crisis situations requiring rapid deployment (e.g., pandemic response).

**Conclusion (1.5 marks):**
The statement is **largely true**. PDSA is not merely a tool but a *management philosophy* aligned with the Deming System of Profound Knowledge. Organizations that institutionalise PDSA outperform those that do not in long-term quality outcomes.

---

### **Question B2** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss the **origin and evolution** of the PDSA cycle. Explain how the cycle differs from traditional project-management approaches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A precision-machining workshop produces bearings with diameter specification **$25.00 \pm 0.05$ mm**. The team runs a PDSA cycle. **Pre-intervention data (n = 30):** $\bar{X}_1 = 25.03$ mm, $s_1 = 0.012$ mm. **Post-intervention data (n = 30):** $\bar{X}_2 = 25.005$ mm, $s_2 = 0.008$ mm. Compute the **process capability index** before and after, and recommend the appropriate ACT-phase decision. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer B2(a) — 7 marks

**Origin and Evolution (3.5 marks):**
The PDSA cycle originated in the 1920s at **Bell Laboratories** through the work of **Walter A. Shewhart**, the father of statistical quality control. Shewhart conceptualised it as a scientific method of iterative learning for industrial processes. In the 1950s, **W. Edwards Deming** popularised and modified the cycle during his lectures in post-WWII Japan, where it became the backbone of Japanese industrial revival. Over the decades, the cycle evolved from a manufacturing tool to a universal improvement framework, adopted by healthcare, education, software (Agile), and service industries. The modern version emphasises *systems thinking* and *psychology* alongside statistical analysis.

**Difference from Traditional Project Management (3.5 marks):**

| Dimension | PDSA Cycle | Traditional Project Mgmt |
|---|---|---|
| Scale | Small, iterative pilots | Big-bang, one-time rollout |
| Learning | Built into every rotation | Limited to post-mortem |
| Risk | Low (small pilot) | High (full-scale) |
| Feedback | Continuous (per cycle) | Delayed (end of project) |
| Tools | SPC, 5 Whys, Fishbone | Gantt, PERT, CPM |
| Philosophy | Learning organisation | Deliverable-focused |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Cue:** When the question says *"critically evaluate"*, the answer *must* contain both an argument and a counter-argument or limitation. A one-sided answer caps at ~5/7 marks.

---

#### Model Answer B2(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify Specification Limits (1 mark):**
USL $= 25.05$ mm, LSL $= 24.95$ mm.

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ Before Intervention (2 marks):**

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{before}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{25.03 - 24.95}{3(0.012)}, \dfrac{25.05 - 25.03}{3(0.012)}\right)
$$

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{before}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{0.08}{0.036}, \dfrac{0.02}{0.036}\right) = \min(2.222, 0.556) = 0.556
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$ After Intervention (2 marks):**

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{25.005 - 24.95}{3(0.008)}, \dfrac{25.05 - 25.005}{3(0.008)}\right)
$$

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{0.055}{0.024}, \dfrac{0.045}{0.024}\right) = \min(2.292, 1.875) = 1.875
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta C_{pk}$ and Decide (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta C_{pk} = 1.875 - 0.556 = 1.319
$$

Since $\Delta C_{pk} = 1.319 \geq 0.33$ **and** $C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = 1.875 \geq 1.00$, the **ACT-phase verdict is ADOPT**. The team should standardise the intervention by updating the SOP, training all operators, and rolling out the change to all production lines. The new baseline becomes the input to the *next* PDSA cycle aimed at pushing $C_{pk}$ toward $2.00$ (Six-Sigma territory).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Computing $C_p$ instead of $C_{pk}$ → loses 1 mark. Always use $C_{pk}$ when the process mean is *not* centred at the target.
> 2. Forgetting the $\min(\cdot)$ function → loses 1 mark.
> 3. Failing to state the ACT decision explicitly → loses 1 mark.
> 4. Not commenting on the centring effect (here, $\bar{X}$ moved from $25.03$ to $25.005$, almost perfectly centred) → loses 0.5 mark for incomplete analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **PDSA = Plan, Do, Study, Act** — the *only* accepted acronym in KTU 2024 scheme.
- Origin: **Shewhart (1920s)** → popularised by **Deming (1950s Japan)**.
- PDSA is the **operational engine of continuous improvement (Kaizen)** and the **Deming philosophy**.
- The cycle is **iterative and recursive** — never terminate after the ACT phase.
- **PLAN** = 5W2H, SMART objective, falsifiable hypothesis with MPSD.
- **DO** = small-scale pilot, baseline data, $n \geq 30$ samples preferred.
- **STUDY** = control charts, $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, hypothesis testing, comparison against MPSD.
- **ACT** = three decisions: **Adopt, Adapt, Abandon** — each triggers a different follow-up.
- Decision rule: $\Delta C_{pk} \geq 0.33$ **and** $C_{pk}^{\text{after}} \geq 1.00$ $\Rightarrow$ **Adopt**.
- Always use $C_{pk}$ (not $C_p$) when the process is **not centred** at the specification midpoint.
- PDSA differs from PDCA: **Study** $>$ **Check** (Study implies deeper analysis and theory-building).
- PDSA differs from traditional project management: **iterative pilots** vs **big-bang rollout**.
- Common tools by phase: PLAN → Pareto, Fishbone, 5W2H; DO → Check sheets, Gantt; STUDY → Control charts, $C_{pk}$; ACT → SOPs, FMEA, training plans.
- The cycle aligns with the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**: PLAN = Apply/Analyze, DO = Apply, STUDY = Evaluate, ACT = Create.
- **Real-world applications** include manufacturing, healthcare, software (Agile), education (NEP 2020), and civil engineering.
- **Common student error:** Terminating the cycle after ACT — examiners explicitly test for recursion awareness.
- **Common student error:** Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ — always pick $C_{pk}$ for off-centre processes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kaizen and Supplier Partnership: Partnering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kaizen and Supplier Partnership: Partnering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Kaizen?

**Kaizen** is a Japanese management philosophy originating from the Toyota Production System, which translates to *"Continuous Improvement"* (改 善 — *Kai* = Change, *Zen* = Good/Better). It is the practice of identifying, implementing, and sustaining small, incremental improvements in processes, products, or services on a continuous basis with the active involvement of **every employee** — from the CEO to the shop-floor worker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Total Quality Management Context):**
> *Kaizen is a Japanese term for "continuous improvement" — a long-term, systematic approach to gradually improve processes, products, and services through small, incremental changes driven by employee involvement at all levels of the organization.*

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Daily Five-Minute Clean-up"**
>
> Imagine you share a kitchen with three flatmates. Every day, instead of waiting for the kitchen to become a complete disaster and then spending a full Saturday cleaning it (the "Big Bang" approach), each flatmate spends just **5 minutes every evening** wiping the counter, organizing the spice rack, and throwing out expired food. Over weeks and months, the kitchen remains perpetually clean, and no single person is ever overwhelmed.
>
> **That "5 minutes daily" habit is Kaizen.** It avoids dramatic, expensive overhauls by stacking tiny wins consistently. In a factory, this might mean a worker noticing that a bolt is positioned awkwardly and suggesting moving it 2 cm closer — saving 3 seconds per cycle. Over 1 million cycles per year, that is **833 hours saved**. That is the power of Kaizen.

---

## 1.2 What is Supplier Partnership?

A **Supplier Partnership** (also called *Strategic Supplier Alliance*) is a long-term, cooperative, win-win relationship between a buyer (customer organization) and its key suppliers, founded on **mutual trust, shared vision, open communication, and joint problem-solving**, rather than the traditional adversarial price-driven "arm's-length" transaction model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Supplier Partnership is a long-term, cooperative relationship between a buyer and a supplier based on mutual trust, openness, shared risk, and shared rewards, designed to leverage the combined capabilities of both organizations to create competitive advantage for the entire supply chain.*

---

## 1.3 What is "Partnering"?

**Partnering** is the *philosophical and operational framework* that transforms a traditional buyer-supplier relationship into a **strategic partnership**. It moves away from multi-supplier, low-bidder, contract-driven purchasing to a **single-source, long-term, integrated relationship** where both parties co-invest in improvement.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy — "The Marriage vs. The One-Night Stand"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
>
> A traditional supplier relationship is like **dating** — you evaluate multiple partners, you keep your wallet close, you switch the moment someone offers a slightly better deal, and there is no emotional investment.
>
> A supplier **partnership** is like a **marriage** — you commit long-term, you share finances, you raise children (products) together, and when one partner faces a crisis (raw material price hike), the other stands by them rather than divorcing.
>
> Partnering is the **wedding ceremony and the vows** that formalize this marriage — the structured process of trust-building, goal-alignment, and reciprocal investment.

---

## 1.4 The Triad: Kaizen + Supplier Partnership + Partnering

These three concepts are **deeply interconnected** in TQM:

| Concept | Role in TQM | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Kaizen** | Engine of continuous improvement | Better processes daily |
| **Supplier Partnership** | Vehicle for extending improvement across the supply chain | Joint value creation |
| **Partnering** | Framework / Strategy | Long-term cooperative model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to understand that *Partnering is the strategy, Supplier Partnership is the structure, and Kaizen is the continuous-improvement philosophy that fuels the partnership once it is established.*

---

## 1.5 Core Pillars of Kaizen (Foundation Vocabulary)

Before diving deep, here are **5 foundational Kaizen pillars** every KTU student must memorize:

1. **Gemba** — "The actual place" where value is created (the shop floor, the call center, the kitchen).
2. **PDCA / Deming Cycle** — **P**lan → **D**o → **C**heck → **A**ct.
3. **5 Whys** — Ask "why" five times to drill to the root cause of a problem.
4. **5S** — **S**ort, **S**et in Order, **S**hine, **S**tandardize, **S**ustain.
5. **Genchi Genbutsu** — "Go and see for yourself" (empirical observation, not reports).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Kaizen

Kaizen is built on **three operational layers**:

### Layer 1 — Philosophy
- Improvement is **never-ending**; no process is ever "perfect."
- Improvements must be **incremental and low-cost** (not capital-intensive).
- **Everyone** participates — from the Managing Director to the housekeeping staff.

### Layer 2 — Tools (The Kaizen Toolbox)

| Tool | Purpose | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **5S** | Workplace organization | Foundation for any improvement |
| **PDCA** | Structured experimentation | When testing any change |
| **5 Whys** | Root-cause analysis | When a problem occurs |
| **Pareto Chart (80/20)** | Priority setting | When many issues exist |
| **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone / Ishikawa)** | Identifying causes | Complex problems |
| **Kanban** | Visual workflow control | Inventory/production flow |
| **Poka-Yoke** | Mistake-proofing | To eliminate human error |
| **Kaizen Blitz / Event** | Rapid 3-5 day improvement sprint | Focused, scoped problems |

### Layer 3 — Cultural Mindset
- *Suggestion systems*, *quality circles*, *gemba walks* by managers, *just-in-time* thinking.

---

## 2.2 Why Kaizen Matters — The Mathematical Logic

Kaizen operates on a deceptively simple exponential-compounding principle. A small daily improvement compounds into massive annual gain.

Let:
- $i$ = daily improvement fraction (e.g., $0.001$ = **0.1 % per day**)
- $n$ = number of working days per year ≈ **250**
- $P_0$ = baseline performance metric

Then after one year:

$$P_{1} = P_{0} \cdot (1 + i)^{n}$$

For $i = 0.001$ and $n = 250$:

$$P_{1} = P_{0} \cdot (1.001)^{250} \approx P_{0} \cdot 1.284$$

That is, a **0.1 % daily improvement** yields a **~28.4 % annual improvement** — *without any massive investment*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the same mathematical structure as **compound interest**. Kaizen is the *interest rate* on operational excellence.

---

## 2.3 Anatomy of Supplier Partnership

A supplier partnership has **six defining structural elements**:

1. **Long-term horizon** (typically 3–10 years, not transactional single orders).
2. **Single-sourcing or limited-sourcing** (not chasing the lowest bidder).
3. **Mutual trust and transparency** (open-book cost-sharing).
4. **Joint problem-solving and innovation**.
5. **Shared risk and shared reward**.
6. **Integration of systems** (EDI, shared ERP, joint quality audits).

---

## 2.4 Partnering — The Strategic Framework

Partnering is the **process** of establishing and sustaining a supplier partnership. The framework is summarized as the **"Partnering Charter"** consisting of:

### 🔸 The Four Pillars of Partnering (Trademark of the Construction Industry Institute, USA — widely cited in TQM literature)

| Pillar | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Mutual Trust** | Willingness to share vulnerabilities and information |
| **Mutual Goals** | Common objectives beyond individual contracts |
| **Mutual Commitment** | Top-management buy-in to long-term success |
| **Mutual Performance Measurement** | Joint KPIs reviewed regularly |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All mathematical vertical bars use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept / Formula | Equation or Definition | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kaizen Compound Improvement Factor | $F = (1 + i)^{n}$ | Dimensionless multiplier |
| 2 | Annual Improvement % | $\Delta P = \left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right] \cdot 100$ | Percent (\%) |
| 3 | Supplier Partnership Duration | $T_{\text{partnership}} \geq 3$ years | Years |
| 4 | Weighted Partner-Selection Score | $S_{j} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} w_{k} \cdot r_{jk}$ | Score, $0 \leq S_j \leq 5$ |
| 5 | Supplier Defect Rate (PPM) | $\text{PPM} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units supplied}} \cdot 10^{6}$ | Parts per million |
| 6 | On-Time Delivery (OTD) % | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Deliveries on time}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \cdot 100$ | Percent (\%) |
| 7 | 5 Whys Depth | Root cause found by $\leq 5$ iterations | Count |
| 8 | PDCA Cycle Time | One full rotation per improvement cycle | Days / Weeks |

Where:
- $i$ = incremental daily improvement fraction (dimensionless)
- $n$ = number of working days
- $w_k$ = weight of criterion $k$ (with $\sum w_k = 1$)
- $r_{jk}$ = rating of supplier $j$ on criterion $k$ (typically 1–5 scale)

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors)**: Kaizen events reduce cycle time by 20–40 % in a week.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, HAL)**: Long-term partnerships with single-tier-1 suppliers (e.g., GE, Rolls-Royce) enable co-engineering.
- **Construction (L&T, Afcons)**: Partnering charters reduced project disputes by ~70 %.
- **IT Services (Infosys, TCS)**: Kaizen applied to ticket resolution and code review.
- **Healthcare (Apollo, AIIMS)**: 5S in operation theatres reduced surgical-site infections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Kaizen Compounding Power

**Problem:** A factory produces 10,000 units/day. A worker proposes a Kaizen that reduces cycle time by 0.05 % per day. Assuming **250 working days/year**, what is the cumulative annual production gain?

**Given:**
- $P_0 = 10{,}000$ units/day
- $i = 0.0005$ (i.e., 0.05 % per day)
- $n = 250$ days

**Step 1 — Apply the Kaizen compounding formula.**

$$\text{Factor } F = (1 + i)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$F = (1 + 0.0005)^{250}$$

**Step 3 — Compute using binomial approximation $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ for small $x$.**

$$\ln(F) = 250 \cdot \ln(1.0005) \approx 250 \cdot 0.0005 = 0.125$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate to recover $F$.**

$$F = e^{0.125} \approx 1.1331$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new daily capacity.**

$$P_{1} = P_{0} \cdot F = 10{,}000 \cdot 1.1331 = 11{,}331 \text{ units/day}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the annual gain.**

$$\Delta P_{\text{annual}} = P_{1} - P_{0} = 1{,}331 \text{ units/day}$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{year}} = 1{,}331 \cdot 250 = 332{,}750 \text{ additional units/year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A seemingly trivial 0.05 % daily improvement yields **~3.33 lakh extra units per year** without any capital expenditure. This is the mathematical proof of Kaizen's power.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Weighted Partner Selection Matrix (Analytical Decision Tool)

**Scenario:** A Kerala-based electronics manufacturer must select one long-term **partner-supplier** for a critical printed-circuit-board (PCB) component. Five candidates (S1 to S5) have been rated on four criteria. Compute the best partner using the weighted-score method.

| Criterion | Weight ($w_k$) | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | S5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality (PPM) | 0.35 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| On-Time Delivery | 0.25 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 4 |
| Cost-Competitiveness | 0.20 | 3 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| Technical Capability | 0.20 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 3 |

(Ratings on a 1–5 scale, where 5 = best.)

**Step 1 — Verify weights sum to unity.**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{4} w_k = 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted score for supplier S1.**

$$S_{1} = (0.35)(4) + (0.25)(5) + (0.20)(3) + (0.20)(4)$$

$$S_{1} = 1.40 + 1.25 + 0.60 + 0.80 = 4.05$$

**Step 3 — Compute score for supplier S2.**

$$S_{2} = (0.35)(3) + (0.25)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(3)$$

$$S_{2} = 1.05 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.60 = 3.65$$

**Step 4 — Compute score for supplier S3.**

$$S_{3} = (0.35)(5) + (0.25)(4) + (0.20)(3) + (0.20)(5)$$

$$S_{3} = 1.75 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 1.00 = 4.35$$

**Step 5 — Compute score for supplier S4.**

$$S_{4} = (0.35)(4) + (0.25)(3) + (0.20)(4) + (0.20)(5)$$

$$S_{4} = 1.40 + 0.75 + 0.80 + 1.00 = 3.95$$

**Step 6 — Compute score for supplier S5.**

$$S_{5} = (0.35)(3) + (0.25)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(3)$$

$$S_{5} = 1.05 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.60 = 3.65$$

**Step 7 — Rank the suppliers.**

| Rank | Supplier | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 🥇 1st | **S3** | **4.35** |
| 2nd | S1 | 4.05 |
| 3rd | S4 | 3.95 |
| 4th (tie) | S2 | 3.65 |
| 4th (tie) | S5 | 3.65 |

**Step 8 — Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{\text{Supplier S3 is selected as the long-term partner.}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** This weighted-score method is the *analytical engine* of partnering — it transforms an emotional "I like vendor X" decision into an objective, defensible, auditable choice. Examiners love this method because it allows both numerical reasoning and management interpretation.

---

## 3.3 The Step-by-Step Partnering Process (Analytical Roadmap)

The full partnering life-cycle, expanded into auditable phases:

### Phase 1 — Strategic Identification
Identify which commodities/services warrant a partnership (typically the top 20 % by spend — **Pareto's 80/20**).

### Phase 2 — Partner Selection
Use the **Weighted Partner Selection Matrix** (Section 3.2).

### Phase 3 — Partnering Charter
Formally draft a **Partnering Charter** — a one-page document signed by both CEOs stating:
- Vision, mission, mutual goals, KPIs, escalation path.

### Phase 4 — Joint Performance Measurement
Set up **Mutual KPIs**: OTD %, PPM defects, cost-reduction, innovation index.

### Phase 5 — Continuous Improvement (Kaizen inside the Partnership)
The supplier is treated as an **extended enterprise**. Joint Kaizen teams visit Gemba together.

### Phase 6 — Review and Renewal
Annual **Partnering Review Meeting** — celebrate wins, identify gaps, renew contract.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Kaizen Engine (PDCA + 5S + Kaizen Event Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Problem at Gemba"]:::start --> B["Apply 5S Workplace Foundation"]
    B --> C["Plan Improvement - PDCA Phase 1"]
    C --> D["Do Small Experiment - PDCA Phase 2"]
    D --> E["Check Results - PDCA Phase 3"]
    E --> F{Qas Improvement Achieved?}
    F -- Yes --> G["Act - Standardize New Method"]
    F -- No --> A
    G --> H["Document in Kaizen Suggestion System"]
    H --> I["Train All Employees"]
    I --> J["Next Gemba Problem Surfaced"]
    J --> A

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph T1["TIER 1 - STRATEGIC PARTNERS"]
        P1["Co-Engineering Partners"]
        P2["Long-Term 5-10 Year Contracts"]
        P3["Open-Book Cost Sharing"]
        P4["Joint Kaizen Teams"]
    end

    subgraph T2["TIER 2 - PREFERRED SUPPLIERS"]

    end

    subgraph T3["TIER 3 - TRANSACTIONAL SUPPLIERS"]
        R1["Spot-Purchase Suppliers"]
        R2["Short-Term PO Basis"]
        R3["Lowest-Bid Driven"]

    end

    T1 -->|Kaizen spillover| T2
    T2 -->|Audit-driven upgradation| T1
    T2 -->|Performance drop| T3
    T3 -->|Performance upgrade| T2

    style T1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style T2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style T3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — The Partnering Process Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["Strategic Sourcing Decision"] --> P1["Phase 1: Identify Critical Commodities via Pareto"]
    P1 --> P2["Phase 2: Shortlist Suppliers via Weighted Matrix"]
    P2 --> P3["Phase 3: Joint Vision Workshop"]
    P3 --> P4["Phase 4: Draft and Sign Partnering Charter"]
    P4 --> P5["Phase 5: Establish Mutual KPIs - OTD, PPM, Cost"]
    P5 --> P6["Phase 6: Deploy Joint Kaizen Events on Gemba"]
    P6 --> P7["Phase 7: Monthly Performance Dashboard"]
    P7 --> P8["Phase 8: Annual Strategic Review and Renewal"]
    P8 --> DECISION{Continue Partnership?}
    DECISION -- Yes --> P6
    DECISION -- No --> P2

    style INPUT fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style DECISION fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — The 5 Whys Root-Cause Logic (Partnering Failure Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Problem: Supplier Delivered Defective Batch"] --> B["Why 1: Why was the batch defective?"]
    B --> C["Operator used wrong solder temperature"]
    C --> D["Why 2: Why was temperature wrong?"]
    D --> E["Machine 7 calibration was 6 months overdue"]
    E --> F["Why 3: Why was calibration overdue?"]
    F --> G["No automated calibration reminder existed"]
    G --> H["Why 4: Why was there no reminder?"]
    H --> I["Maintenance schedule not integrated with ERP"]
    I --> J["Why 5: Why was ERP not integrated?"]
    J --> K["ROOT CAUSE: Partnering lacked joint IT-system integration clause in charter"]
    K --> L["COUNTER-MEASURE: Add shared ERP dashboard to Partnering Charter"]

    style K fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

# 🔷 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Kaizen. List any four Kaizen tools.**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Kaizen is a Japanese management philosophy meaning *"continuous improvement"*. It is a long-term, systematic approach in which **all employees** — from top management to shop-floor workers — actively participate in making **small, incremental improvements** to processes, products, and services on an ongoing basis.
>
> **Four Kaizen Tools:**
> 1. **5S** — Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain (workplace organization).
> 2. **PDCA Cycle** — Plan, Do, Check, Act (structured experimentation).
> 3. **5 Whys** — Repeated "why" questioning to identify root causes.
> 4. **Poka-Yoke** — Mistake-proofing devices that prevent human error.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for tools)*

---

### **Q2. What is meant by "Supplier Partnership"? Mention two key benefits.**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Meaning:** A supplier partnership is a **long-term, cooperative, strategic alliance** between a buyer and a key supplier, based on **mutual trust, open communication, shared risk, and shared rewards**, designed to leverage combined capabilities for creating competitive advantage across the supply chain.
>
> **Two Key Benefits:**
> 1. **Cost Reduction** — Joint Kaizen activities and volume commitments lower per-unit cost.
> 2. **Improved Quality & Innovation** — Long-term horizon enables co-engineering and joint problem-solving, driving higher PPM standards and faster product innovation.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for meaning + 0.75 each for benefits)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain the **Kaizen philosophy** in detail. Discuss its **key principles** and illustrate with a suitable **engineering example**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **concept of Partnering**. Explain the **four pillars of partnering** and the **steps involved in implementing a partnering relationship** between a buyer and a supplier. *(7 marks)*

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, End Semester, Adapted]`

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(a)

**Kaizen Philosophy:**

Kaizen is a Japanese term meaning **continuous improvement**. It is a long-term, people-centric management philosophy originating from the Toyota Production System. It emphasizes that improvement is **everyone's responsibility**, every day, in every part of the organization.

**Key Principles:**

1. **Process-Oriented Thinking** — Focus on improving the *process*, not blaming the person.
2. **Small-Step Incremental Improvement** — Prefer many tiny changes over one big disruptive change.
3. **Gemba-Based Decisions** — Decisions are made after observing the actual workplace.
4. **Employee Empowerment** — Workers closest to the process are best positioned to improve it.
5. **PDCA Discipline** — All improvements go through Plan-Do-Check-Act.
6. **Standardization** — Once improved, lock the new method as the new standard.

**Engineering Example:**

> At a Kerala-based PCB assembly plant, **defect rate** was 5,000 PPM due to solder-bridge defects. A Kaizen team at Gemba applied **5 Whys** and discovered that flux was being applied unevenly because the operator could not see the through-hole clearly.
>
> A small **Kaizen suggestion** — installing a **3x magnification lamp** at the workstation (cost: ₹800) — was piloted using **PDCA**. After one month, PPM dropped from 5,000 to 1,200 (a 76 % reduction). The new standard was then propagated to all 12 workstations, saving the company approximately **₹18 lakh/year** in rework and scrap.

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(b)

**Concept of Partnering:**

Partnering is the **strategic process** of transforming a traditional adversarial buyer-supplier relationship into a **collaborative, long-term, win-win alliance**. It shifts the focus from **price-per-unit** to **total-value-creation**.

**Four Pillars of Partnering** (Construction Industry Institute framework):

| # | Pillar | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mutual Trust** | Both parties willingly share vulnerabilities, costs, and forecasts |
| 2 | **Mutual Goals** | A joint mission statement beyond individual profit |
| 3 | **Mutual Commitment** | Top-management sponsorship and resource allocation |
| 4 | **Mutual Performance Measurement** | Joint KPIs reviewed in regular forums |

**Steps in Implementing Partnering:**

1. **Strategic Identification** — Use Pareto analysis to identify top 20 % critical commodities.
2. **Partner Selection** — Apply weighted scoring matrix (quality, OTD, cost, capability).
3. **Joint Vision Workshop** — Both teams align on goals, scope, and risks.
4. **Partnering Charter** — Sign a formal charter document at top-management level.
5. **Joint KPIs** — Establish OTD %, PPM defects, cost-reduction targets.
6. **Joint Kaizen** — Deploy cross-functional Kaizen teams on supplier Gemba.
7. **Monthly Dashboard Reviews** — Transparent performance tracking.
8. **Annual Strategic Review** — Renew or restructure the partnership.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **PDCA (Deming) Cycle** as a Kaizen tool. Describe how it is applied to drive **continuous improvement** in a manufacturing organization. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **concept of Supplier Partnership**. Discuss the **key differences between traditional purchasing and partnership-based sourcing**, using a comparative table. *(7 marks)*

`[KTU University Exam — May 2023, End Semester, Adapted]`

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(a)

**PDCA Cycle — Block Diagram (text representation):**

```
        ┌─────────────────────────┐
        │       PLAN             │
        │ Identify problem,      │
        │ analyze root cause,    │
        │ design countermeasure  │
        └───────────┬─────────────┘
                    ▼
        ┌─────────────────────────┐
        │        DO               │
        │ Pilot the change on     │
        │ a small scale           │
        └───────────┬─────────────┘
                    ▼
        ┌─────────────────────────┐
        │       CHECK             │
        │ Measure results vs.     │
        │ planned targets         │
        └───────────┬─────────────┘
                    ▼
        ┌─────────────────────────┐
        │       ACT               │
        │ Standardize if success; │
        │ else restart cycle      │
        └───────────┬─────────────┘
                    │
                    └──────► (Loop back to PLAN)
```

**Application in Manufacturing:**

- **PLAN:** A defect is identified (e.g., paint bubbles). The team uses **fishbone diagram + 5 Whys** to find that humidity in the paint booth is uncontrolled.
- **DO:** Install a hygrometer and humidity controller on a single booth for 2 weeks.
- **CHECK:** Defects drop from 8 % to 1.5 %. Statistical process control (SPC) confirms significance.
- **ACT:** The new humidity SOP is rolled out to all 6 booths. The team then returns to PLAN for the next defect (Kaizen cycle repeats).

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(b)

**Concept of Supplier Partnership:** *(Already defined in Section 1.2 — reproduce with examples specific to this answer.)*

**Comparative Table — Traditional Purchasing vs. Partnership-Based Sourcing:**

| Dimension | Traditional Purchasing | Partnership-Based Sourcing |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Short-term, transactional | Long-term (3–10 years) |
| **Supplier Count** | Many suppliers, competitive bidding | Few or single-source strategic suppliers |
| **Basis of Selection** | Lowest price (L1) | Total value (quality + cost + reliability) |
| **Communication** | Adversarial, formal, arm's-length | Open-book, transparent, joint |
| **Risk** | Borne mainly by supplier | Shared between both parties |
| **Information Sharing** | Minimal — kept confidential | Full — forecasts, costs, roadmaps shared |
| **Improvement Focus** | Price negotiation each cycle | Joint Kaizen and continuous improvement |
| **Conflict Resolution** | Legal / contractual | Mutual problem-solving forum |
| **Innovation** | Rarely co-developed | Joint R&D and co-engineering |
| **Relationship Mindset** | Win-Lose | Win-Win |

*(Award full 7 marks if at least 8 distinct comparison rows are presented with brief justification.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Kaizen with Innovation** — Kaizen = small, continuous, employee-led. Innovation = big, disruptive, R&D-led. Mixing them is a **2-mark penalty** in many valuation schemes.
> 2. **Writing "Supplier Partnership" without examples** — KTU examiners expect at least one real-world example (Toyota, Tata, L&T, Boeing). A purely textbook answer scores only 60–70 % of the available marks.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why" of partnering** — Merely listing the four pillars without explaining *why each matters* loses marks for "application-level" thinking (Bloom's Level 3).
> 4. **Not drawing a block diagram** — In 7-mark sub-parts, a diagram (PDCA / partnering process) is **mandatory** and carries approximately 2 marks by itself.
> 5. **Ignoring the 5S foundation** — Many students jump straight to PDCA without mentioning 5S. Examiners expect the **5S → Kaizen → PDCA** logical sequence.
> 6. **Calculation errors in weighted-score problems** — Always verify that the weights sum to **1.00** before computing. A common trap: forgetting to normalize.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Kaizen & Partnering**

### 🔹 Kaizen
- ✅ Japanese for "continuous improvement."
- ✅ Originated from Toyota Production System.
- ✅ **Three layers:** Philosophy + Tools + Culture.
- ✅ **Five pillars:** Gemba, PDCA, 5 Whys, 5S, Genchi Genbutsu.
- ✅ **Compound-effect principle:** $(1+i)^{n}$.
- ✅ Driven by **every employee**, every day.

### 🔹 Supplier Partnership
- ✅ Long-term, strategic, win-win buyer-supplier alliance.
- ✅ Based on **trust, transparency, shared risk, shared reward**.
- ✅ Replaces adversarial, L1-bid, short-term purchasing.
- ✅ Partner treated as **extended enterprise**.
- ✅ Open-book cost-sharing and joint R&D.

### 🔹 Partnering
- ✅ The *strategic framework* that creates supplier partnerships.
- ✅ **Four pillars:** Mutual Trust, Mutual Goals, Mutual Commitment, Mutual Performance Measurement.
- ✅ Documented in a **Partnering Charter** signed by both CEOs.
- ✅ Eight-step life-cycle: Identification → Selection → Charter → KPIs → Joint Kaizen → Dashboard → Annual Review → Renewal.

### 🔹 High-Yield Vocabulary (Examiner Triggers)
- ✅ *Gemba* — actual place of work.
- ✅ *Poka-Yoke* — mistake-proofing.
- ✅ *Kanban* — visual signal.
- ✅ *Kaizen Blitz* — focused 3–5 day event.
- ✅ *Jidoka* — autonomy with intelligence (stop-on-defect).
- ✅ *Muda, Mura, Muri* — waste, unevenness, overburden (3 enemies of Kaizen).

### 🔹 Formula Snapshot
- ✅ $F = (1 + i)^{n}$
- ✅ $S_j = \sum_{k=1}^{m} w_k \cdot r_{jk}$
- ✅ $\text{PPM} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units}} \cdot 10^{6}$
- ✅ $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{On-time deliveries}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \cdot 100$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sourcing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sourcing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Sourcing** in Total Quality Management (TQM) is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, selecting, and managing suppliers (vendors) who can provide the right materials, components, and services of the right quality, at the right price, at the right time, and in the right quantity to support an organization's value chain. It is the upstream quality gateway that determines the inherent quality of every input flowing into the production system.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus, sourcing is a **strategic supplier management activity** that transforms the traditional buyer–seller relationship into a collaborative partnership focused on continuous quality improvement, cost reduction, and innovation.

### Key Terminology Table

| Term | KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Sourcing** | The end-to-end process of finding, qualifying, and engaging suppliers. |
| **Supplier/Vendor** | An external party that provides goods, materials, or services to the organization. |
| **Inherent Quality** | The quality of a product or service that is built into it at the design or input stage. |
| **Strategic Sourcing** | A long-term, data-driven approach to supplier selection and relationship management. |
| **Outsourcing** | Contracting an external supplier to perform activities previously done in-house. |
| **Single Sourcing** | Procurement from one approved supplier only. |
| **Multiple Sourcing** | Procurement from two or more competing suppliers. |
| **Co-Manufacturing** | Collaborative production where supplier and buyer jointly create value. |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍳

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy: The Master Chef and the Spice Merchant**  
> Imagine a master chef running a 5-star restaurant. The chef can be the world's best cook, but if the spices are stale, the vegetables are wilted, and the rice is broken — **no amount of cooking brilliance can fix bad inputs**. The chef therefore spends enormous time **carefully choosing** the spice merchant, visiting the farm, checking the harvest, signing **long-term contracts** with the best suppliers, and sometimes even teaching the farmer **how to grow better spices**.  
> This is exactly **Sourcing** in TQM — quality cannot be "inspected in" later, it must be **sourced in** from the very beginning of the supply chain.

### Why Sourcing is Critical in TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Core TQM Principle:** *"Quality of the output is determined by the quality of the input."*  
> In Japanese: **"Genchi Genbutsu"** — *go to the source*.  
> Sourcing embodies this principle by ensuring that every component entering the production line meets defined quality, cost, and delivery standards.

**Three Strategic Roles of Sourcing:**

1. **Quality Assurance at Source** — preventing defects before they enter the system (Poka-Yoke at the supplier interface).
2. **Cost Optimization** — total cost of ownership (TCO) reduction, not just purchase price.
3. **Risk Mitigation** — supply continuity, geopolitical hedging, and quality risk distribution.

### Standard Metrics Used in Sourcing (KTU Highlight)

- **PPM (Parts Per Million)** — defect rate at supplier level
- **OTD (On-Time Delivery)** — delivery reliability percentage
- **SQR (Supplier Quality Rating)** — composite supplier score
- **LTC (Lead Time Consistency)** — variation in delivery time
- **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** — true cost beyond unit price

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Sourcing Process — Structured Logic Flow

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures sourcing into **7 sequential steps**:

**Step 1 — Identify Sourcing Need**
- Determine what materials, services, or components are required.
- Specify quality, quantity, cost, and delivery (QQCD) requirements.
- Internal stakeholders: Engineering, Production, Quality, Finance.

**Step 2 — Market Research & Supplier Identification**
- Scan the supplier market using trade directories, exhibitions, e-sourcing platforms.
- Build a long-list of potential suppliers (typically 10–20 candidates).

**Step 3 — Supplier Pre-Qualification**
- Eliminate obviously unsuitable suppliers using minimum criteria.
- Common filters: financial stability, capacity, certifications (ISO 9001, ISO 14001, IATF 16949).

**Step 4 — Supplier Evaluation & Selection**
- Apply weighted scoring models (mathematical formula below).
- Use tools: Vendor Rating, Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP), Cost-Benefit Analysis.

**Step 5 — Negotiation & Contracting**
- Establish quality clauses, penalty/incentive structures.
- Define service-level agreements (SLAs) and key performance indicators (KPIs).

**Step 6 — Supplier Development & Continuous Improvement**
- Joint quality improvement programs (e.g., kaizen at supplier site).
- Supplier audits, training, and capability-building.

**Step 7 — Performance Monitoring & Review**
- Track supplier performance using metrics (PPM, OTD, SQR).
- Periodic business reviews (quarterly/annual) with suppliers.

### Types of Sourcing Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

| Strategy | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Sourcing** | One supplier for a part | Strong partnership, lower TCO, simplified logistics | High dependency, supply risk |
| **Multiple Sourcing** | Several suppliers for same part | Risk diversification, competitive pricing | Higher admin cost, inconsistent quality |
| **Dual Sourcing** | Two qualified suppliers | Balanced risk & partnership | Moderate complexity |
| **Strategic Sourcing** | Long-term partnership with key suppliers | Innovation, joint cost reduction | Requires trust and information sharing |
| **Outsourcing** | External supplier performs entire function | Focus on core competency | Loss of control, quality risk |
| **Insourcing** | Bring activity back in-house | Full control, IP protection | High capital investment |
| **Global Sourcing** | Suppliers from across the world | Cost arbitrage, access to technology | Currency risk, long lead times, cultural barriers |
| **Local Sourcing** | Suppliers within the same region | Short lead time, easier communication | Limited capability, higher cost |
| **Partnership Sourcing** | Co-development, shared risk/reward | Innovation, mutual growth | Slow decision making |
| **Co-Manufacturing** | Joint production with supplier | Capacity flexibility | Coordination overhead |
| **JIT Sourcing** | Just-in-time delivery | Low inventory, fresh quality | Vulnerable to disruption |
| **E-Sourcing** | Online procurement platforms | Transparency, broad reach | Digital divide issues |

### Supplier Selection Criteria (Weighted Model)

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the **weighted-point supplier rating method** as the primary analytical tool:

- **Quality** (e.g., defect PPM, certification)
- **Cost** (unit price + TCO components)
- **Delivery** (on-time, lead time, flexibility)
- **Capability** (technical competence, capacity, R&D)
- **Reliability** (financial health, past performance)
- **Responsiveness** (communication, support, problem resolution)

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> **Critical formulas for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) — remember these!**

**1. Supplier Rating (Weighted Point Method):**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \times r_i$$

Where:
- $S$ = Total supplier score
- $w_i$ = Weight of the $i^{th}$ criterion (in decimals, $\sum w_i = 1$)
- $r_i$ = Rating of supplier on the $i^{th}$ criterion (typically 0–10 scale)
- $n$ = Number of criteria

**2. Supplier Quality Rating (SQR) — Defect-based:**

$$SQR = \frac{Accepted\ Lots}{Total\ Lots\ Inspected} \times 100\%$$

**3. Parts Per Million (PPM) Defect Rate:**

$$PPM = \frac{Number\ of\ Defective\ Units}{Total\ Units\ Received} \times 10^6$$

**4. On-Time Delivery (OTD) Percentage:**

$$OTD = \frac{Number\ of\ On\text{-}Time\ Deliveries}{Total\ Deliveries} \times 100\%$$

**5. Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):**

$$TCO = C_{purchase} + C_{logistics} + C_{inventory} + C_{quality} + C_{failure} + C_{admin}$$

**6. Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) at Supplier:**

$$COPQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure}$$

**7. Defect Probability (Poisson Approximation for Sourcing Risk):**

$$P(x) = \frac{\lambda^x \cdot e^{-\lambda}}{x!}$$

Where $\lambda$ is the expected defect rate.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Sourcing directly impacts:
- **Automotive Industry** — IATF 16949 mandates supplier quality management.
- **Electronics** — component sourcing drives PCB reliability.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — API sourcing affects drug safety and efficacy.
- **Aerospace** — AS9100 requires full traceability of sourced parts.
- **Construction** — material sourcing affects structural integrity and project timelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state the link between sourcing and TQM principles (customer focus, continuous improvement, supplier partnership) when answering 14-mark questions. Examiners reward answers that show **systems thinking**.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Example 1: Supplier Selection Using Weighted Point Method

**Problem:** A KTU-evaluated company needs to select a supplier for a critical component. After pre-qualification, **three suppliers** (A, B, C) are evaluated on **four criteria**. Determine the best supplier.

| Criterion | Weight ($w_i$) | Supplier A ($r_A$) | Supplier B ($r_B$) | Supplier C ($r_C$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.40 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Cost | 0.25 | 7 | 6 | 9 |
| Delivery | 0.20 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Capability | 0.15 | 6 | 8 | 8 |

**Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Verify the weights sum to 1 (validity check):

$$\sum w_i = 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Apply the supplier rating formula for Supplier A:

$$S_A = w_1 r_{A1} + w_2 r_{A2} + w_3 r_{A3} + w_4 r_{A4}$$

$$S_A = (0.40 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 6)$$

$$S_A = 3.20 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 0.90$$

$$S_A = 7.65$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula for Supplier B:

$$S_B = (0.40 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 8)$$

$$S_B = 3.60 + 1.50 + 1.60 + 1.20$$

$$S_B = 7.90$$

**Step 4:** Apply the formula for Supplier C:

$$S_C = (0.40 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8)$$

$$S_C = 2.80 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.20$$

$$S_C = 7.65$$

**Step 5:** Compare scores:

$$S_B = 7.90 > S_A = S_C = 7.65$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conclusion:** **Supplier B** is selected as it has the highest weighted score (7.90), primarily because of its strong quality rating (9/10) and high capability (8/10), which align with TQM emphasis on quality at source.

### Worked Example 2: Supplier Quality Rating (SQR) and PPM Calculation

**Problem:** A company received **8 shipments** from a supplier. A total of **12,500 components** were inspected, of which **75 were found defective**. **7 shipments** were accepted. Calculate SQR, PPM, and OTD if out of 8 deliveries, **6 were on time**.

**Step 1: Calculate SQR**

$$SQR = \frac{Accepted\ Lots}{Total\ Lots\ Inspected} \times 100\%$$

$$SQR = \frac{7}{8} \times 100\% = 87.50\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate PPM Defect Rate**

$$PPM = \frac{Defective\ Units}{Total\ Units\ Received} \times 10^6$$

$$PPM = \frac{75}{12{,}500} \times 10^6$$

$$PPM = 0.006 \times 10^6 = 6000\ PPM$$

**Step 3: Calculate OTD Percentage**

$$OTD = \frac{On\text{-}Time\ Deliveries}{Total\ Deliveries} \times 100\%$$

$$OTD = \frac{6}{8} \times 100\% = 75.00\%$$

**Step 4: Interpretation Matrix**

| Metric | Value | Industry Benchmark (Automotive) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| SQR | 87.50% | > 95% | ⚠️ Below Standard |
| PPM | 6,000 | < 100 (for critical) | ❌ Poor |
| OTD | 75.00% | > 98% | ❌ Critical |

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Many students forget to **multiply by $10^6$** in the PPM formula. Also, students confuse SQR (lot-level) with PPM (unit-level) — they are **two different metrics**.

### Worked Example 3: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Comparison

**Problem:** Compare two suppliers based on TCO for an annual requirement of 50,000 units.

| Cost Component | Supplier X (₹) | Supplier Y (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Unit Purchase Price | 100 | 95 |
| Freight (annual) | 50,000 | 80,000 |
| Inventory Carrying | 30,000 | 60,000 |
| Quality Inspection | 20,000 | 10,000 |
| Rejection / Returns | 40,000 | 5,000 |
| Administrative | 10,000 | 15,000 |
| **Total Purchase** | 50,000 × 100 = 50,00,000 | 50,000 × 95 = 47,50,000 |

**Step 1: TCO for Supplier X**

$$TCO_X = 50{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 10{,}000$$

$$TCO_X = ₹51{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 2: TCO for Supplier Y**

$$TCO_Y = 47{,}50{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 15{,}000$$

$$TCO_Y = ₹49{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 3: Compare TCO vs. Purchase Price**

| Aspect | Supplier X | Supplier Y |
|---|---|---|
| Purchase Price | ₹50,00,000 (Higher) | ₹47,50,000 (Lower) |
| **TCO** | **₹51,50,000 (Higher)** | **₹49,20,000 (Lower)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Insight:** Supplier Y wins on **both** purchase price AND TCO. However, if only purchase price was considered, Supplier Y would still win here. In real KTU cases, the cheaper unit-price supplier can have a **higher TCO** due to quality, logistics, and failure costs. This is the classic TQM lesson: **"Buy on TCO, not on unit price."**

### Analytical Matrix — Sourcing Decision Flowchart Logic

$$\text{Critical Component?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Single Sourcing (Strategic Partner)}$$
$$\downarrow \text{No}$$
$$\text{High Volume?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Multiple Sourcing with Framework Agreement}$$
$$\downarrow \text{No}$$
$$\text{Time-Sensitive?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Local Sourcing / JIT Sourcing}$$
$$\downarrow \text{No}$$
$$\text{Standardized Item?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{E-Sourcing / Catalog Buying}$$
$$\downarrow \text{No}$$
$$\text{Innovative Need?} \rightarrow \text{Partnership Sourcing / Co-Manufacturing}$$

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Sourcing Process Flow (TQM-Integrated)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Identify Sourcing Need QQCD Requirements"] --> B["Step 2: Market Research Long List Suppliers"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Pre Qualification Financial and Certification Check"]

    D --> E["Step 5: Negotiation and Contracting SLA and Quality Clauses"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Supplier Development Kaizen and Capability Building"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Performance Monitoring PPM OTD SQR Review"]

    H -- No --> J["Corrective Action or Re sourcing"]
    J --> B
```

### Diagram 2: Sourcing Strategy Selection Tree

    H -- No --> J["E Sourcing Catalog Buying"]

    F --> K

```

### Diagram 3: Supplier Relationship Continuum (KTU Concept)

```

### Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of TQM Sourcing System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Engineering Specifications"]
        A2["Quality Standards ISO IATF AS"]
        A3["Cost and Volume Targets"]
    end
    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        B1["Supplier Search Engine"]
        B2["Evaluation Engine Weighted Scoring"]
        B3["Audit and Monitoring Engine"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1["Qualified Supplier List"]
        C2["Supplier Performance Dashboard"]
        C3["Continuous Improvement Reports"]
    end
    subgraph FEED["FEEDBACK LAYER"]
        D1["PPM and SQR Feedback"]
        D2["OTD and Cost Variance Feedback"]
        D3["Customer Complaint Traceability"]
    end
    IN --> PROC
    PROC --> OUT
    OUT --> FEED
    FEED --> PROC
```

### Diagram 5: TCO Components Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Cost of Ownership"] --> B["Purchase Price"]
    A --> C["Logistics Cost"]
    A --> D["Inventory Carrying Cost"]
    A --> E["Quality Cost"]
    A --> F["Failure Cost"]
    A --> G["Administrative Cost"]
    B --> B1["Unit Price Multiplied by Volume"]
    C --> C1["Freight Insurance Customs"]
    D --> D1["Storage Obsolescence Capital"]
    E --> E1["Inspection Testing Rejection"]
    F --> F1["Warranty Returns Downtime"]
    G --> G1["PO Processing Communication"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Define sourcing. List any **four** types of sourcing strategies used in TQM.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Sourcing is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, selecting, and managing suppliers to provide materials, components, and services of the right quality, cost, time, and quantity that align with organizational TQM goals.
>
> **Four Types (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Single Sourcing** — procurement from one approved supplier.
> 2. **Multiple Sourcing** — procurement from several competing suppliers.
> 3. **Strategic Sourcing** — long-term data-driven partnership approach.
> 4. **Outsourcing** — contracting external suppliers for activities previously done in-house.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**  
What is the **Supplier Quality Rating (SQR)**? Write its formula.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Supplier Quality Rating (SQR) is a lot-acceptance-based metric that measures the percentage of supplier shipments accepted by the buyer after incoming inspection. It reflects the supplier's consistency in delivering acceptable quality lots.
>
> **Formula (1 mark):**
> $$SQR = \frac{Accepted\ Lots}{Total\ Lots\ Inspected} \times 100\%$$

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **seven-step sourcing process** in TQM with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is evaluating **two suppliers** for an electronic component. The weights and ratings are as follows:

| Criterion | Weight | Supplier P | Supplier Q |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.35 | 9 | 8 |
| Cost | 0.30 | 7 | 9 |
| Delivery | 0.20 | 8 | 7 |
| Service | 0.15 | 6 | 8 |

Using the **weighted point method**, determine which supplier should be selected. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The seven-step sourcing process in TQM is as follows:

1. **Identify Sourcing Need** (1 mark): Define the QQCD requirements — Quality, Quantity, Cost, Delivery. This involves coordinating with engineering, production, and quality teams.

2. **Market Research and Supplier Identification** (1 mark): Identify potential suppliers through trade directories, exhibitions, e-sourcing platforms, and industry referrals. Build a long list of 10–20 candidates.

3. **Supplier Pre-Qualification** (1 mark): Apply minimum qualifying criteria such as ISO 9001 certification, financial stability, capacity, and regulatory compliance. Eliminate obviously unsuitable suppliers.

4. **Supplier Evaluation and Selection** (1 mark): Use weighted scoring models, AHP, or cost-benefit analysis to evaluate remaining suppliers objectively.

5. **Negotiation and Contracting** (1 mark): Establish quality clauses, SLAs, penalty/incentive structures, and dispute resolution mechanisms.

6. **Supplier Development** (1 mark): Conduct joint kaizen events, training programs, and capability-building activities to upgrade supplier competencies.

7. **Performance Monitoring and Review** (1 mark): Track KPIs such as PPM, OTD, and SQR. Conduct periodic business reviews and recognize high performers.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Diagram credit:** Reference the **Sourcing Process Flow** diagram from Section 4 of these notes for full marks.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Verify Weights**

$$\sum w_i = 0.35 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 2: Calculate Score for Supplier P**

$$S_P = (0.35 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 6)$$

$$S_P = 3.15 + 2.10 + 1.60 + 0.90 = 7.75$$

**Step 3: Calculate Score for Supplier Q**

$$S_Q = (0.35 \times 8) + (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8)$$

$$S_Q = 2.80 + 2.70 + 1.40 + 1.20 = 8.10$$

**Step 4: Comparison and Decision**

$$S_Q = 8.10 > S_P = 7.75$$

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** **Supplier Q** is selected because it has the higher weighted score (8.10). Although Supplier P has better quality and delivery, Supplier Q's superior cost and service ratings give it the overall edge.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** — [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]

**(a)** Compare **Single Sourcing** and **Multiple Sourcing** strategies. Under what conditions is each strategy most appropriate? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A supplier delivered **5 lots** of 2,000 units each. Inspection results: Lot 1 had 6 defects, Lot 2 had 8 defects, Lot 3 had 4 defects, Lot 4 had 10 defects, Lot 5 had 2 defects. The buyer accepts lots with **PPM ≤ 3000**. Determine which lots are accepted. Calculate the **overall PPM** and **SQR**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Aspect | Single Sourcing | Multiple Sourcing |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** (0.5 m) | One approved supplier | Several competing suppliers |
| **Risk** (0.5 m) | High — full dependency | Low — risk distributed |
| **Cost** (0.5 m) | Lower TCO, volume discounts | Higher admin, lower unit price |
| **Quality** (0.5 m) | Strong partnership, deep collaboration | Inconsistent, harder to standardize |
| **Innovation** (0.5 m) | High — co-development | Moderate — competitive pressure |
| **Logistics** (0.5 m) | Simplified, JIT friendly | Complex coordination |
| **When to use** (4 × 0.5 = 2 m) | **Single:** Critical components, strategic items, long lifecycle, high switching cost | **Multiple:** Standardized parts, low criticality, supply chain risk, regulatory requirements |

**Appropriate Conditions:**

- **Single Sourcing is best for:** critical safety components, proprietary technology, JIT systems, when supplier has unique capability, and when long-term partnership is desired for innovation.
- **Multiple Sourcing is best for:** standardized commodities, low-value items, when supply risk is high, when the buyer needs bargaining leverage, and in regulated industries requiring vendor diversity.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Calculate PPM per Lot**

Each lot contains 2,000 units. Acceptable PPM ≤ 3,000 means **acceptable defects per lot ≤ 6**.

$$Acceptable\ Defects\ per\ Lot = \frac{3000 \times 2000}{10^6} = 6\ defects$$

| Lot | Defects | PPM | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6 | $\frac{6}{2000} \times 10^6 = 3000$ | ✅ Accepted (boundary) |
| 2 | 8 | $\frac{8}{2000} \times 10^6 = 4000$ | ❌ Rejected |
| 3 | 4 | $\frac{4}{2000} \times 10^6 = 2000$ | ✅ Accepted |
| 4 | 10 | $\frac{10}{2000} \times 10^6 = 5000$ | ❌ Rejected |
| 5 | 2 | $\frac{2}{2000} \times 10^6 = 1000$ | ✅ Accepted |

**Step 2: Overall PPM**

$$Overall\ PPM = \frac{Total\ Defects}{Total\ Units} \times 10^6$$

$$Overall\ PPM = \frac{6 + 8 + 4 + 10 + 2}{5 \times 2000} \times 10^6 = \frac{30}{10{,}000} \times 10^6 = 3000\ PPM$$

**Step 3: Supplier Quality Rating (SQR)**

$$SQR = \frac{Accepted\ Lots}{Total\ Lots} \times 100\% = \frac{3}{5} \times 100\% = 60\%$$

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** Lots 1, 3, and 5 are accepted. Overall PPM is **3000** (at boundary), and SQR is **60%** — both indicating the supplier is **not meeting** the desired quality threshold and requires corrective action.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Unit mistake in PPM:** Forgetting the $10^6$ multiplier is the #1 error. Always show full calculation.  
> 2. **SQR confusion:** SQR is a **lot-level** metric, while PPM is a **unit-level** metric. Do NOT mix them up.  
> 3. **Weighted sum verification:** Always verify $\sum w_i = 1$ before computing the supplier score — failing to do so loses **0.5 marks** instantly.  
> 4. **TCO vs. Purchase Price:** In 14-mark questions, students often compute only the purchase price and ignore logistics, inventory, and failure costs. This shows a **lack of TQM understanding** and can cost up to **3 marks**.  
> 5. **Diagram omission:** A sourcing process question without a diagram loses **1–2 marks**. Always include at least a flowchart.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sourcing in TQM**

- ✅ **Definition:** Sourcing = strategic supplier identification, evaluation, selection, and management.
- ✅ **Core Principle:** Quality of output is determined by quality of **input** (Genchi Genbutsu).
- ✅ **Seven-Step Process:** Need → Market Research → Pre-Qualification → Evaluation → Contracting → Development → Monitoring.
- ✅ **Key Strategies:** Single, Multiple, Dual, Strategic, Outsourcing, Insourcing, Global, Local, Partnership, Co-Manufacturing, JIT, E-Sourcing.
- ✅ **Weighted Point Formula:** $S = \sum w_i \times r_i$, with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- ✅ **SQR Formula:** $SQR = \frac{Accepted\ Lots}{Total\ Lots} \times 100\%$ (lot-level).
- ✅ **PPM Formula:** $PPM = \frac{Defects}{Total\ Units} \times 10^6$ (unit-level).
- ✅ **OTD Formula:** $OTD = \frac{On\text{-}Time\ Deliveries}{Total\ Deliveries} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **TCO Components:** Purchase + Logistics + Inventory + Quality + Failure + Admin.
- ✅ **COPQ at Supplier:** Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
- ✅ **Single vs. Multiple Sourcing:** Trade-off between partnership depth and supply risk.
- ✅ **TQM Linkage:** Sourcing is upstream of production — it embeds quality, cost, and reliability into the value chain.
- ✅ **Industry Standards:** ISO 9001, IATF 16949 (automotive), AS9100 (aerospace), ISO 14001 (environmental).
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** Buy on **TCO**, not on unit price alone.
- ✅ **Modern Trend:** Strategic sourcing, supplier partnership, digital e-sourcing platforms.
- ✅ **Exam Mnemonic:** **"QCDC"** — Quality, Cost, Delivery, Capability (the four pillars of supplier evaluation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supplier Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supplier Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Supplier Selection** is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, qualifying, and approving external vendors or partners who can supply materials, components, or services that meet an organization's quality, cost, delivery, and reliability requirements consistently and continuously. In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, supplier selection is not merely a procurement activity — it is a **strategic extension of the quality system** governed by the principle *"Quality is built into the product at every stage, including incoming materials."*

According to the KTU 2024 scheme framework, supplier selection is treated as a sub-component of **Vendor Management / Supplier Quality Management**, which is an integral pillar of TQM alongside Customer Focus, Process Management, and Continual Improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition**
> *Supplier Selection* is the structured, multi-criteria decision-making process used by an organization to identify, evaluate, rank, and approve suppliers based on their ability to meet predefined quality, cost, delivery, and service parameters in alignment with the organization's TQM objectives.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **hiring a chef for your home kitchen**. You would not pick the first cook who walks in. Instead, you would:
- Test their cooking (Quality)
- Compare their fees (Cost)
- Check if they show up on time (Delivery / Reliability)
- See if they accommodate your special dietary needs (Flexibility)
- Verify their hygiene and food-handling practices (Process Capability)
- Ask for references and reviews (Reputation / Past Performance)

**Supplier Selection in TQM works exactly the same way.** The organization acts as the "homeowner", and suppliers are the "chefs". The process is methodical, evidence-based, and long-term oriented because the supplier's quality becomes **your product's quality** in the eyes of the customer.

### 1.3 Importance in TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Supplier Selection is Critical in TQM**
> 1. **Incoming Quality:** Defects from suppliers propagate downstream and become your defects.
> 2. **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ):** Bad suppliers inflate inspection, rework, warranty, and recall costs.
> 3. **Customer Satisfaction:** A substandard component can destroy brand reputation built over decades.
> 4. **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compatibility:** Reliable suppliers are the backbone of JIT systems.
> 5. **Strategic Partnership:** Long-term supplier relationships foster co-development, innovation, and mutual growth.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used (Highlighted)

The following are the universally accepted **KTU-aligned supplier selection metrics**, all of which carry equal weight in TQM theory:
- **Quality** (Incoming quality level, PPM defects)
- **Cost** (Price competitiveness, total cost of ownership)
- **Delivery** (On-time delivery percentage, lead-time adherence)
- **Capability** (Technical, process, and financial capability)
- **Service** (Responsiveness, warranty, after-sales support)
- **Flexibility** (Volume flexibility, mix flexibility)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Supplier Selection Process — Step-by-Step Logic

A structured supplier selection process typically involves **seven sequential stages**. Each stage has a clear "why" and "how" that aligns with TQM principles.

1. **Identify the Requirement** — *Why:* You cannot select a supplier without knowing what you need. *How:* Define the material/service specification, quantity, quality standards, and delivery expectations.
2. **Identify Potential Suppliers** — *Why:* Create a competitive pool. *How:* Use market surveys, trade directories, referrals, supplier exhibitions, and pre-qualification questionnaires.
3. **Pre-Qualification Screening** — *Why:* Eliminate unsuitable candidates early to save time. *How:* Check financial stability, regulatory compliance, and basic capability.
4. **Detailed Evaluation (Solicitation & Analysis)** — *Why:* Convert subjective judgment into objective data. *How:* Send RFQ (Request for Quotation), request samples, conduct on-site audits, and gather performance data.
5. **Final Selection / Award Decision** — *Why:* Make a transparent, data-driven choice. *How:* Use weighted scoring models, cost-based analysis, or categorical methods.
6. **Supplier Development & Onboarding** — *Why:* Selected supplier must integrate with your QMS. *How:* Share quality manuals, agree on SOPs, define communication channels, and establish joint performance metrics.
7. **Continuous Performance Monitoring & Feedback** — *Why:* TQM is a journey, not a one-time event. *How:* Track supplier scorecards, conduct periodic reviews, drive continual improvement via **PDCA cycles**.

### 2.2 Methods of Supplier Evaluation (KTU High-Yield)

The following evaluation methods are examinable in KTU 2024 ESE:

#### 2.2.1 Categorical Method
Suppliers are classified into **three categories**:
- **Preferred Suppliers** (Excellent) — Long-term contracts awarded.
- **Approved Suppliers** (Satisfactory) — Approved for normal orders.
- **Marginal Suppliers** (Needs Improvement) — Approved with conditions, monitored closely.

#### 2.2.2 Weighted Point Method (Most Important for Numericals)
Each evaluation criterion is assigned a **weight** based on its strategic importance. The supplier is scored on a fixed scale (commonly 1 to 10). The final weighted score is calculated, and the highest scorer wins.

#### 2.2.3 Cost Ratio Method
Compares suppliers based on the ratio of **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** to the **Purchase Price (PP)**:

$$
\text{Cost Ratio} = \frac{\text{Total Cost of Ownership}}{\text{Purchase Price}}
$$

A **lower cost ratio** indicates a more cost-effective supplier.

#### 2.2.4 Vendor Rating (Vendor Performance Index)
A continuous monitoring method where suppliers are rated periodically using:

$$
\text{Vendor Rating} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times S_i)
$$

where $W_i$ is the weight of the $i$-th criterion and $S_i$ is the score of the $i$-th criterion.

#### 2.2.5 Supplier Certification
Long-term recognition awarded to suppliers who consistently meet stringent quality benchmarks, allowing reduced incoming inspection.

### 2.3 Sourcing Decisions

| Strategy | Description | TQM Suitability |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Single Sourcing** | One supplier for a given item | Risky, but enables deep partnership |
| **Multiple Sourcing** | Several suppliers for the same item | Safer, competitive, but harder to standardize quality |
| **Dual Sourcing** | Two qualified suppliers | Balanced approach |
| **Outsourcing** | External partner handles entire process | Strategic for non-core activities |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mathematically verifiable and aligned with KTU 2024 TQM syllabus.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Weighted Score | $\text{WS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times S_i)$ | Dimensionless, scale-based |
| 2 | Normalized Weight | $W_i = \frac{\text{Weight of criterion } i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}$ | Sum of all $W_i$ = 1 |
| 3 | Cost Ratio | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{TCO}}{\text{PP}}$ | Lower is better |
| 4 | On-Time Delivery % | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{On-time deliveries}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 5 | Incoming Quality (PPM) | $\text{PPM} = \dfrac{\text{Defective units}}{\text{Units supplied}} \times 10^6$ | Parts per million |
| 6 | Vendor Rating | $\text{VR} = \sum (W_i \times S_i)$ | Same as Weighted Score |
| 7 | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | Monetary (INR/USD) |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolated Pipes:** Note that in the table above, vertical bars were avoided using `\vert` style to prevent markdown table corruption. Always use `\mid` or `\vert` in exam answers too.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Supplier selection frameworks are not academic exercises — they are the **backbone of global supply chains**:
- **Automotive (Toyota, Ford):** Strict supplier quality standards (e.g., Q1, ISO/TS certifications).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** FDA-validated suppliers are mandatory for GMP compliance.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus):** AS9100-certified suppliers form the supply chain.
- **Electronics (Apple, Samsung):** Multi-tier supplier audits are performed globally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example: Weighted Point Method (Full Derivation)

A company wants to select a supplier for precision-machined components. The following criteria and weights have been finalized by the TQM steering committee. Three suppliers (A, B, C) are evaluated on a 1–10 scale.

**Step 0 — Problem Setup:**

| Criterion | Weight (out of 100) | Supplier A Score | Supplier B Score | Supplier C Score |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Quality    | 40 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Cost       | 25 | 7 | 6 | 9 |
| Delivery   | 20 | 9 | 8 | 6 |
| Service    | 10 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| Flexibility| 5  | 7 | 6 | 8 |

**Step 1 — Normalize the Weights (Sum of Weights = 100).**

$$
W_{\text{Quality}} = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40
$$

$$
W_{\text{Cost}} = \frac{25}{100} = 0.25
$$

$$
W_{\text{Delivery}} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20
$$

$$
W_{\text{Service}} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10
$$

$$
W_{\text{Flexibility}} = \frac{5}{100} = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Weighted Score for Each Supplier.**

For Supplier A:

$$
\text{WS}_A = (0.40 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 6) + (0.05 \times 7)
$$

$$
\text{WS}_A = 3.20 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 0.60 + 0.35 = 7.70
$$

For Supplier B:

$$
\text{WS}_B = (0.40 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.05 \times 6)
$$

$$
\text{WS}_B = 3.60 + 1.50 + 1.60 + 0.80 + 0.30 = 7.80
$$

For Supplier C:

$$
\text{WS}_C = (0.40 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.05 \times 8)
$$

$$
\text{WS}_C = 2.80 + 2.25 + 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.40 = 7.35
$$

**Step 3 — Rank the Suppliers.**

$$
\text{WS}_B = 7.80 > \text{WS}_A = 7.70 > \text{WS}_C = 7.35
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

**Supplier B should be selected** because it has the highest weighted score (7.80 out of 10).

### 3.2 Worked Example: Cost Ratio Method

Suppose Supplier A quotes a piece price of **INR 100** for a component, but the company estimates **INR 15** of additional cost (inspection, rework, warranty, downtime). Supplier B quotes **INR 110** with estimated additional cost of **INR 6**.

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).**

$$
\text{TCO}_A = 100 + 15 = 115
$$

$$
\text{TCO}_B = 110 + 6 = 116
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Cost Ratio.**

$$
\text{CR}_A = \frac{115}{100} = 1.15
$$

$$
\text{CR}_B = \frac{116}{110} \approx 1.0545
$$

**Step 3 — Decision.**

Although Supplier A appears cheaper at first glance, **Supplier B has a lower cost ratio (1.0545 < 1.15)**, making it the more cost-effective choice when total ownership cost is considered.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Method

| Method | Best For | Limitation |
|--------|----------|------------|
| Categorical | Quick screening, small supplier base | Subjective |
| Weighted Point | Strategic decisions, multi-criteria | Requires accurate weights |
| Cost Ratio | Cost-driven decisions | Ignores non-cost factors |
| Vendor Rating | Ongoing performance tracking | Requires historical data |
| Certification | Long-term high-value partnerships | Resource-intensive |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
